topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a unified hybrid AI framework that integrates time-resolved cryo-EM, NMR relaxation data, and AlphaFold static predictions to generate high-resolution dynamic protein structural ensembles capturing conformational transitions and transient states.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art methods like AlphaFold provide high-accuracy static protein structures but cannot model protein conformational dynamics essential for understanding function, allostery, and transient intermediate states. Experimental techniques such as time-resolved cryo-EM and NMR offer complementary temporal and dynamic constraints but lack effective computational integration with AI-based static predictions. The open problem is to combine these heterogeneous temporal experimental datasets into a single computational model that accurately infers protein motions and structural ensembles at high resolution.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid deep generative model architecture that fuses static 3D coordinates predicted by AlphaFold2 with time-resolved cryo-EM density maps (e.g., from the Cryo-EM Time-Resolved Dataset) and NMR relaxation dispersion data (e.g., from the Biological Magnetic Resonance Bank). The model uses a temporal graph neural network to encode dynamic constraints from cryo-EM frames and NMR-derived conformational exchange rates, jointly optimized with a variational autoencoder that generates ensembles consistent with both static and dynamic data. Training will leverage supervised learning on proteins with known dynamic structures (e.g., ribosome conformational states, adenylate kinase) and use a multi-modal loss combining reconstruction accuracy, temporal consistency, and experimental fit metrics.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark datasets including the Time-Resolved Cryo-EM Dataset, Biological Magnetic Resonance Bank (BMRB) relaxation dispersion entries, and CASP14 dynamic targets. Baselines include AlphaFold2, DeepTracer-LowResEnhance, and DeepFragLib. Metrics include: (1) ensemble RMSD improvement over static predictions (target >1.5 Å absolute reduction), (2) improved cross-correlation coefficients (CC) between predicted and experimental cryo-EM maps (>5% increase over DeepTracer-LowResEnhance), and (3) enhanced agreement with NMR relaxation parameters (reduction of χ² by >15%). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs over ~10 million training steps, with 5 random seeds and extensive ablation on each modality’s contribution.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior approaches that either model static structures (AlphaFold2) or separately analyze cryo-EM and NMR data, this method integrates heterogeneous time-resolved experimental modalities with AI predictions in a single end-to-end framework to directly infer protein conformational ensembles and temporal transitions at high resolution, addressing the critical bottleneck of dynamic structural modeling unmet by existing methods.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate physics-guided anisotropic membrane potentials into a multi-scale graph neural network that jointly models transmembrane protein folding and lipid bilayer dynamics to improve structural accuracy and domain orientation beyond current membrane-aware predictors.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning protein structure predictors, including AlphaFold2 and recent physics-guided graph neural networks, inadequately model membrane proteins by treating their environments as isotropic aqueous solvents, leading to inaccurate domain orientations and unrealistic folding inside lipid bilayers. This failure hinders understanding of membrane protein structure-function relationships and limits predictive power for transmembrane regions, which require explicit modeling of anisotropic membrane physics and lipid-protein interactions.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-scale physics-guided graph neural network (PG-GNN) architecture that fuses amino acid-level graphs with coarse-grained lipid bilayer models, incorporating anisotropic energy potentials derived from membrane biophysics directly into the training loss. This includes explicit orientation-dependent terms for hydrophobic mismatch, lipid headgroup interactions, and membrane curvature effects, learned jointly with residue contact and angle predictions. The network will be trained end-to-end to optimize both folding accuracy and membrane embedding realism, using differentiable physics-informed energy functions coupled with geometric deep learning on protein and membrane graphs.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the MemProtMD, OPM (Orientations of Proteins in Membranes), and PDBTM (Protein Data Bank of Transmembrane proteins) benchmark datasets. Baselines include AlphaFold2, RoseTTAFold, and the Physics-Guided Graph Neural Networks Incorporating Membrane Anisotropy method (the tournament winner). Metrics: achieve >15% improvement in membrane domain orientation accuracy (measured by tilt and rotation angle deviation), >10% reduction in RMSD of transmembrane domains, and >5% improvement in predicted hydrophobic mismatch scores. Conduct ablations on energy term contributions and multi-scale integration. Utilize 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a training budget of ~3 weeks, performing 5 independent runs for statistical validation.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior models that treat membrane proteins as embedded in isotropic solvents or incorporate static anisotropic potentials, this method uniquely combines multi-scale lipid bilayer modeling with end-to-end learnable anisotropic physics potentials within a graph neural network framework. This enables simultaneous optimization of protein folding and membrane environment representation, yielding more physically realistic transmembrane domain orientations and folding than either purely data-driven or physics-agnostic approaches.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Develop a generative graph neural network framework that explicitly models the full dynamic conformational ensembles of intrinsically disordered proteins (IDPs) by integrating temporal graph attention and physics-informed regularization to predict IDP structural heterogeneity beyond static snapshots like AlphaFold.

BACKGROUND: AlphaFold and related static protein structure predictors fail to capture the intrinsic conformational heterogeneity of IDPs due to their reliance on well-defined folded structures, leaving a critical gap in understanding the dynamic ensembles that underpin IDP function. The open problem is how to accurately represent and predict IDP structural ensembles as distributions of transient, rapidly interconverting states rather than single static conformations, which is essential for elucidating their roles in signaling, regulation, and disease.

APPROACH: We propose a generative graph neural network architecture that models IDPs as temporally evolving residue graphs with dynamic edge weights and node states encoding conformational fluctuations. The model employs temporal graph attention layers to capture time-dependent interactions and conformational transitions, combined with physics-informed energy-based loss functions enforcing biophysical plausibility (e.g., avoidance of steric clashes, realistic bond length distributions). The network is trained to generate diverse, realistic conformational ensembles conditioned on sequence and experimental constraints such as NMR chemical shifts or SAXS profiles.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on established IDP benchmark datasets including DisProt, Protein Ensemble Database (PED), and the AlphaFold Protein Structure Database’s predicted disorder regions. Baselines include AlphaFold2, DeepFragLib, and recent IDP ensemble predictors like Flexible-Meccano. Metrics: Achieve >15% improvement in ensemble coverage measured by RMSD distribution overlap with experimental ensembles, >10% improvement in conformer diversity (Shannon entropy of generated ensembles), and achieve <2 Å average deviation in SAXS profile fits compared to baselines. Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a training budget of ~30 GPU-days, using 5 independent seeds for statistical significance. Ablation studies will isolate contributions of temporal attention and physics-informed loss terms.

NOVELTY: Unlike AlphaFold and DeepFragLib, which produce static or limited ensemble representations, this method explicitly models the temporal evolution and full conformational landscape of IDPs, integrating experimental data and physics constraints to generate biophysically plausible, diverse structural ensembles. This fills the critical gap of capturing IDP dynamic disorder beyond fixed topologies, enabling new mechanistic insights inaccessible to current predictors.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA: Develop a compressed, physics-informed protein folding model leveraging multi-level knowledge distillation combined with mixed-precision quantization to enable sub-200MB on-device inference with <1.5 Å RMSD degradation, targeting personalized protein structure prediction on ARM-based wearable and mobile platforms.

BACKGROUND: Current physics-informed protein folding models that integrate structural and biophysical constraints achieve high accuracy but exceed the memory and compute limits of mobile and wearable devices, preventing practical in situ personalized protein structure prediction for precision medicine. Existing compression approaches primarily focus on data-driven models, lacking preservation of interpretability and physical constraints, which are critical for trustworthy predictions in constrained environments.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-stage compression pipeline that applies knowledge distillation at the architecture, feature, and output levels from a large, physics-constrained teacher model to a compact student network, followed by mixed-precision quantization optimized for ARM processors. This pipeline preserves physics-based constraints by embedding differentiable energy terms into the student training objective and employs structured pruning guided by physical interpretability metrics. The end-to-end model supports real-time, personalized inference with memory usage under 200MB and RMSD within 1.5 Å of the teacher.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on ProteinNet, CASP14 dataset, and the CAMEO continuous evaluation benchmark to measure structure prediction accuracy and generalizability. Baselines include AlphaFold2, Physics-informed Neural Networks (PINNs) for folding, and the Compressed physics-informed protein folding models (the tournament winner). Metrics include RMSD (target <1.5 Å degradation vs teacher), memory footprint (<200MB on ARM Cortex-A77), inference latency (<500 ms), and energy consumption reductions (>30% vs baseline). Conduct ablation on distillation levels, quantization bit-widths, and pruning granularity across 5 seeded runs on embedded ARM hardware.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that applies compression solely to data-driven models or offers physics-informed models too large for edge devices, this method ensures aggressive compression while explicitly preserving physical constraints and interpretability through multi-level distillation and structured pruning. This enables on-device personalized protein structure prediction with rigorously validated accuracy and efficiency, a capability absent from current SOTA.

topic: Protein structure prediction beyond AlphaFold
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hybrid generative framework combining equivariant graph neural networks and normalizing flows to model full protein conformational ensembles reflecting functional dynamics, enabling improved molecular docking and drug discovery beyond static AlphaFold predictions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current protein structure prediction methods, including AlphaFold2, provide highly accurate static snapshots but fail to capture the biologically relevant conformational dynamics critical for drug binding and allosteric regulation. This lack of dynamic structural ensembles limits the accuracy of molecular docking and impedes iterative drug design workflows that depend on understanding protein flexibility. The core challenge is generating diverse, experimentally consistent conformations efficiently without prohibitive computational cost.

APPROACH:  
We propose a two-stage generative model: first, an SE(3)-equivariant graph neural network (EGNN) encodes a protein’s static structure and biochemical context to learn a latent representation of conformational variability; second, a conditional normalizing flow model samples diverse backbone and side-chain conformations consistent with known biophysical constraints and experimental data (e.g., NMR ensembles). This framework integrates multiple data modalities, including Protein Ensemble Database (PED) NMR ensembles and Cryo-EM multi-state datasets, to supervise and regularize dynamics generation. The generated ensembles will be validated via docking score improvements and conformational diversity metrics.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark on the Protein Ensemble Database (PED), the NMR Restraints Data Bank (NRDB), and the CASP14 multistate target sets. Baselines include AlphaFold2, RoseTTAFold, and DeepFragLib fragment-based sampling. Metrics will comprise ensemble RMSD coverage against experimental states (>15% improvement over baselines), docking score improvements on DUD-E targets (>10% average affinity prediction gain), and computational efficiency (at least 2x faster sampling vs MD simulations). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with five seeds per condition and ablation studies isolating flow vs EGNN contributions.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike current methods that generate single conformations or rely on expensive molecular dynamics, this approach jointly learns spatially equivariant latent spaces and efficient normalizing flows to produce diverse, functionally relevant protein conformations at scale. This surpasses DeepFragLib’s fragment libraries and static AlphaFold predictions by explicitly modeling conformational ensembles with interpretable dynamics, directly closing the gap between structure prediction and practical drug discovery needs.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an adaptive reinforcement learning framework for real-time quantum error correction on NISQ devices that dynamically optimizes error suppression policies by leveraging fine-grained hardware noise profiling and low-latency feedback, achieving superior logical qubit fidelity with minimal qubit and computational overhead.

BACKGROUND:  
Near-term NISQ devices encounter highly dynamic and heterogeneous noise sources that degrade qubit coherence and gate fidelity, limiting the effectiveness of conventional quantum error correction (QEC) methods which require substantial qubit overhead and static noise assumptions. Existing adaptive machine learning approaches for error suppression focus primarily on classical noise characterization or fixed correction strategies that cannot quickly adjust to transient noise fluctuations or hardware variability under strict latency constraints. A method is needed that can learn and adapt quantum error correction policies on-the-fly by integrating real-time hardware noise data while minimizing resource overhead.

APPROACH:  
We propose a reinforcement learning (RL) agent embedded within the quantum control stack that continuously interacts with a quantum device’s noise environment, using hardware-specific noise datasets (e.g., IBM Quantum Experience noise profiles, Rigetti Aspen noise logs, and Google Sycamore error maps) as training and evaluation benchmarks. The RL agent employs a low-complexity policy network optimized for rapid inference on NISQ control hardware, dynamically selecting error-correcting operations and adaptive decoding strategies tailored to current noise conditions. The system incorporates online noise characterization via quantum tomography-inspired protocols and uses reward signals derived from logical qubit fidelity and gate error rates to refine policies in real time. This framework is experimentally validated by integrating with three baselines: the adaptive ML approach from “Adaptive Machine Learning for Error Suppression in Near-Term NISQ Experimental Platforms” (2027), the “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices” (2026), and “Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design” (2026), aiming to show >10% absolute logical error rate reduction, sub-millisecond decision latency, and a qubit overhead reduction of at least 20%.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark datasets comprising IBM Qiskit Noise Models, Rigetti’s Aspen-11 noise datasets, and Google Sycamore calibration and error logs. Metrics include logical qubit fidelity (%) improvement (>10% improvement vs baselines), correction decision latency (<1 ms per cycle), and qubit overhead reduction (>20% fewer qubits than surface code equivalents). Experiments run on simulated NISQ devices with noise injection and on real hardware via cloud access, over 5 random seeds and ablation studies isolating policy network complexity and noise characterization frequency under a fixed compute budget of 500 GPU hours.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing adaptive ML solutions that rely on offline training or static noise assumptions, our RL-based framework enables continual, real-time policy adaptation to transient noise fluctuations with explicit latency and overhead constraints, achieving a practical balance unaddressed by prior work. Our approach integrates hardware noise profiling directly into the error correction policy learning loop, a capability absent from both static QEC codes and prior adaptive ML methods, enabling new levels of robustness and efficiency for NISQ-era quantum error correction.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop an integrated ultra-low latency quantum error correction framework combining FPGA-accelerated syndrome decoding with adaptive quantum compiler strategies to dynamically optimize error correction codes and gate operations in real time on NISQ devices.

BACKGROUND: Current quantum error correction (QEC) schemes for NISQ devices struggle to balance error suppression with the overhead and latency introduced by syndrome measurement and decoding, which traditionally rely on software-based methods causing delays incompatible with dynamically fluctuating noise. Existing FPGA-accelerated syndrome extraction methods reduce latency but do not adapt dynamically to changing error rates or optimize error correction protocols inline with gate scheduling and qubit mapping. This static approach limits practical error suppression in real-world NISQ environments where noise profiles evolve rapidly.

APPROACH: We propose a hybrid hardware-software system where FPGA modules perform ultra-low latency syndrome extraction and decoding (<1 µs), tightly coupled with an adaptive quantum compiler backend that dynamically selects and adjusts low-overhead QEC codes (such as the recent 10-qubit codes from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices”) and optimizes qubit mapping and gate sequences in real time based on decoded error syndromes. The compiler uses real-time telemetry from superconducting qubit sensors integrated with the FPGA to inform on-the-fly code switching and gate noise mitigation strategies, enabling continuous feedback control with minimal latency overhead.

EXPERIMENT: We will benchmark on the IBM Quantum Device Benchmark Dataset (QDBD), Rigetti Quantum Cloud Services Noise Profiles, and Google Sycamore Error Telemetry Logs. Baselines for comparison include Ultra-Low Latency Syndrome Extraction with FPGA Acceleration (2027), Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices (2026), and Error-Resilient Quantum Compiler Design (2026). Evaluation metrics include total syndrome extraction and decoding latency (target <1 µs), logical qubit error rate reduction (aim >15% improvement versus baseline codes), and end-to-end sensor fidelity enhancement (target >10% increase). Experiments will run on a 32-qubit superconducting platform with FPGA integration, using 10 independent noise realizations and 5 random seeds for statistical significance. Ablations will test FPGA decoding alone, adaptive compiler alone, and combined system.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that isolates FPGA-accelerated decoding or compiler-based noise mitigation, this research uniquely integrates them into a closed-loop system enabling real-time adaptive QEC code selection and gate optimization informed by ultra-low latency syndrome feedback. This dynamic adaptivity and hardware-software co-design surpass static FPGA decoding schemes and offline compiler optimizations, directly addressing latency and noise evolution challenges in NISQ quantum error correction.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop and deploy a lightweight, hardware-aware machine learning decoder for quantum error correction in NISQ devices that achieves real-time, low-latency decoding with minimal qubit overhead and energy consumption, enabling practical error correction on resource-constrained embedded quantum systems.

BACKGROUND: NISQ devices suffer from high noise and decoherence, making quantum error correction (QEC) essential but challenging due to the large qubit overhead and slow decoding latency of existing QEC codes like surface codes. Current ML-based decoders provide accuracy but are computationally intensive and unsuitable for low-power embedded quantum systems, creating a bottleneck in real-time error correction. There is a critical need for QEC decoders that operate within strict timing and energy constraints while maintaining robust error suppression on NISQ-scale devices.

APPROACH: We propose designing a novel embedded ML-based quantum error decoder using compressed neural architectures (e.g., quantized spiking neural networks or binarized transformers) that exploits hardware-aware pruning and approximate computing techniques tailored to low-power FPGAs and ASICs. The decoder will directly interface with physical qubit syndrome measurements to perform fast inference under tight latency (<10 µs) and power (<100 mW) budgets. Additionally, we integrate a lightweight adaptive feedback mechanism that dynamically adjusts decoding parameters to NISQ device noise profiles and qubit connectivity constraints, reducing qubit overhead by up to 40% compared to standard surface code implementations.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark quantum error correction datasets including the IBM Quantum Experience Surface Code syndrome datasets, Google Sycamore QEC syndrome traces, and Rigetti Aspen error syndrome logs. Compare against baselines: Minimum-Weight Perfect Matching (MWPM), Union-Find decoder, and Neural Belief Propagation (NBP) decoders. Metrics include logical error rate (target < 0.005), decoding latency (target <10 µs), power consumption (target <100 mW on embedded hardware), and qubit overhead reduction (target >30% fewer qubits). Experiments will be run on embedded FPGA platforms (e.g., Xilinx Zynq UltraScale) with 5 independent seeds and ablation studies on model compression and feedback adaptation components.

NOVELTY: Unlike previous ML decoders that prioritize accuracy but disregard latency and embedded resource constraints, our approach is the first to co-design the ML decoder architecture and embedded hardware deployment for real-time QEC in NISQ devices. It achieves a novel balance of low latency, low power, and reduced qubit overhead that prior work on surface codes, low-overhead QEC codes, or ML decoders cannot. This enables practical, scalable error correction on embedded quantum systems where existing decoders fail due to computational and energy bottlenecks.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hybrid model-based and compressed sensing quantum error correction protocol with real-time dynamic noise tracking and sparse adaptive feedback control that achieves sub-10% logical error rates on NISQ devices while reducing memory overhead by over 60% compared to Memory-Efficient Adaptive Error Correction for Dynamic Noise Profiles.

BACKGROUND:  
Current adaptive quantum error correction (QEC) methods for edge NISQ devices, such as the Memory-Efficient Adaptive Error Correction for Dynamic Noise Profiles, reduce memory overhead via sparse representations but still face limitations in adapting rapidly fluctuating noise with minimal qubit and classical resource usage. Existing low-overhead QEC codes (e.g., tailored surface or repetition codes) provide low logical error rates but do not integrate dynamic noise adaptation with memory efficiency, nor do they handle real-time feedback in highly non-stationary noise environments seen in, for example, NV-center quantum sensors or superconducting transmon qubits.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid adaptive QEC framework combining (1) model-based noise characterization using Kalman filtering for continuous noise parameter estimation, (2) compressed sensing to represent noise profiles in a dynamically updated sparse basis, and (3) a lightweight feedback controller that adapts syndrome decoding thresholds and recovery operations in real time based on the compressed noise state. The method exploits temporal correlations in noise fluctuations, enabling predictive error correction updates with minimal classical memory and computational overhead. This approach integrates with low-overhead surface or Bacon-Shor codes optimized for NISQ architectures.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark quantum noise datasets: (1) NV-center magnetic resonance noise profiles from the Electron Spin Resonance Benchmark Dataset (ESR-2022), (2) IBM Quantum Experience superconducting qubit noise logs (IBMQ-NISQ-2024), and (3) simulated fluctuating amplitude-damping and phase-damping channels generated via Qiskit Aer’s noise modeling. Baselines include Memory-Efficient Adaptive Error Correction for Dynamic Noise Profiles, Surface code with static noise assumption, and Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for NISQ Devices. Metrics will measure (a) logical error rate reduction (>10% absolute improvement over baseline adaptive QEC), (b) memory footprint reduction (target >60% vs Memory-Efficient Adaptive Error Correction), and (c) adaptation latency (sub-millisecond response to noise changes). Experiments will run on simulated NISQ hardware models with up to 20 physical qubits, using 10 independent random seeds and ablation studies varying noise fluctuation rates and sparse basis dimensionality.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods that either focus solely on memory reduction or low-overhead codes without dynamic adaptation, this approach uniquely combines predictive model-based noise tracking with compressed sensing and real-time adaptive feedback, enabling significantly better error rate suppression and lower memory usage in rapidly changing noise environments—capabilities unachievable by Memory-Efficient Adaptive Error Correction or fixed low-overhead QEC codes alone.

topic: Quantum error correction in NISQ devices
text:
IDEA: Develop a hybrid quantum-classical error correction framework that integrates efficient low-overhead QEC codes with advanced probabilistic noise modeling and adaptive quantum compiler optimizations to enable scalable, high-fidelity quantum computations on NISQ devices running deep circuits.

BACKGROUND: Current quantum error correction (QEC) techniques for NISQ devices face two critical challenges: prohibitively high qubit overhead for fully fault-tolerant codes like surface codes, and insufficient error resilience in low-overhead alternatives, especially for deep circuits with complex, structured noise. Additionally, existing error mitigation and compiler-level optimizations do not sufficiently leverage QEC codes and noise models jointly, limiting effective noise suppression in scientific simulations and other demanding workloads.

APPROACH: We propose a three-pronged method: (1) employ recently developed low-overhead QEC codes optimized for NISQ constraints (e.g., the 10-qubit optimized code from “Low-Overhead Quantum Error Correction Codes for Noisy Intermediate-Scale Quantum Devices”); (2) integrate these with sophisticated probabilistic noise inference models inspired by “Near-Term Quantum Devices for Scientific Simulations” that model structured, correlated noise without incurring excessive classical overhead; (3) incorporate an adaptive quantum compiler layer that dynamically maps logical qubits and gates to physical hardware constrained by noise patterns and QEC code structure, improving gate fidelity and reducing logical error rates in deep circuits.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate the framework on benchmark quantum simulation problems using the Quantum Chemistry Benchmark Suite (QCBench), the IBM Quantum Experience datasets (e.g., IBM Qiskit Pulse calibration data), and the Rigetti Aspen noise profiles. Baselines include the surface code implementation, the 10-qubit low-overhead QEC code alone, and state-of-the-art error mitigation via zero-noise extrapolation and probabilistic error cancellation. Metrics: logical error rate reduction targeting <0.005 compared to baselines, gate fidelity improvement by >15%, and simulation accuracy improvements measured by energy estimation errors reduced by >10% absolute on QCBench molecules. Experimental protocols include testing on IBM and Rigetti cloud QPUs with at least 5 experimental runs per configuration and ablations isolating the impact of each system component.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that treats QEC codes, noise inference, and compiler optimizations as separate modules, this research tightly couples low-overhead QEC with real-time probabilistic noise modeling and adaptive compilation, enabling error correction efficiency and noise mitigation synergistically in deep circuits on NISQ devices—a capability that current state-of-the-art methods, which either suffer high overhead or fail to scale to deep circuits, cannot deliver.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA: Formulate and empirically validate inverse scaling laws for large language models explicitly constrained by edge device limitations on memory, compute, and energy, enabling optimized model-data-resource trade-offs for real-time on-device deployment.

BACKGROUND: Current scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) optimize performance primarily under unconstrained or cloud-scale compute budgets, ignoring strict edge device constraints such as limited memory capacity (e.g., <8GB RAM), bounded energy consumption, and latency requirements. This results in models that are infeasible for real-time, on-device inference or training. The open problem is to develop scaling laws that incorporate these hard hardware limits to guide the design of LLMs that maximize task performance while respecting latency and resource budgets on edge hardware.

APPROACH: We propose an inverse problem formulation of scaling laws that jointly optimize model size, dataset size, and compute under explicit hardware constraints: memory (peak RAM usage ≤8GB), energy (battery consumption < 10Wh per inference), and latency (<100ms per query). Our method incorporates hardware-specific cost functions into the scaling objective, leveraging Pareto frontier analysis and multi-objective optimization. We train and evaluate model configurations on edge-simulated hardware setups, using quantized and architectural efficiency methods such as pruning and low-rank adaptation to explore the feasible scaling regime. The resulting laws specify how to allocate compute and data resources to achieve near-optimal performance within edge constraints.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on natural language understanding tasks using GLUE, SuperGLUE, and SQuAD 2.0 datasets. Baselines include BERT-base, DistilBERT, and MobileBERT models, as well as GPT-2 small for autoregressive language modeling. Quantitative metrics include F1 score improvements >2% over MobileBERT on GLUE, SQuAD exact match increases by 3%, latency reductions >30% vs GPT-2 small on edge hardware simulators (e.g., Raspberry Pi 4, NVIDIA Jetson), and peak memory usage below 8GB. Experiments will run with fixed compute budgets ≤500M FLOPs/inference, with 5 seeds per configuration and ablations on model size, quantization levels, and dataset sizes.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work on inverse scaling laws that do not explicitly incorporate strict edge device constraints, our method integrates hard memory, latency, and energy budgets into the scaling law formulation, producing actionable, hardware-aware scaling targets. This enables principled model and data scaling for real-time edge deployment, which is not addressed by existing unconstrained or cloud-centric approaches.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Leverage probabilistic models of heterogeneous large-scale data distributions combined with explicit computational cost constraints to jointly optimize LLM scaling parameters—model size, data size, and training duration—maximizing cost-efficiency measured by perplexity per dollar on real-world heterogeneous datasets.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for large language model training optimize model size and data scale primarily for performance without explicitly incorporating cost constraints or the rich heterogeneity of real-world training data distributions. Existing cost-aware approaches either ignore complex data distribution characteristics or fail to jointly optimize compute budget, data complexity, and scaling parameters, leading to suboptimal resource utilization and missed opportunities for improved efficiency in large-scale LLM training.

APPROACH:  
We propose a cost-aware scaling parameter optimization framework that models large-scale data using probabilistic mixture models capturing distribution heterogeneity (e.g., domain diversity, linguistic complexity) from datasets like The Pile and C4. This model is integrated with an explicit compute cost function reflecting cloud GPU/TPU pricing and training efficiency dynamics on AWS EC2 clusters. Using this joint model, we solve a constrained optimization problem that balances model size, data size, and training steps to minimize perplexity per dollar. The method incorporates adaptive batch scheduling and dynamic data sampling guided by data complexity indicators to further improve cost-effectiveness during batch training.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on The Pile, C4, and WikiText-103 datasets using GPT-2 base and GPT-3 medium as baselines, alongside AdamW and LoRA optimizers. Report perplexity, total training cost in USD, and perplexity per dollar spent, targeting at least 10% improvement in cost-efficiency over baseline scaling schedulers. Experimental protocol includes training on AWS EC2 p4d instances with a fixed compute budget of 1000 GPU-hours, running 3 independent seeds for statistical significance, and conducting ablations on components such as data mixture complexity modeling and cost-function constraints.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works such as “Uncovering Scaling Laws via Inverse Problems” or generic cost-aware methods, this approach explicitly models the rich heterogeneity in large-scale data distributions and jointly optimizes scaling parameters with realistic compute cost models. This enables principled, practical cost-efficiency improvements in LLM training that prior scaling laws or cost models—focusing solely on compute or data scale—do not achieve.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a unified, data-driven scaling law framework that jointly models the energy-latency-quality trade-offs of Large Language Models (LLMs) during real-time interactive inference by integrating hardware transient power profiles, latency distributions, and response quality metrics to identify optimal scaling points under strict energy and latency constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for LLMs primarily focus on performance improvements or training efficiency without explicitly addressing the combined impact of energy consumption and latency during real-time interactive use. Existing optimization methods like "Energy-Latency Joint Optimization for Real-Time Interactive LLMs" model hardware power and latency but do not incorporate predictive scaling laws linking model size, architecture, and compute budget to these joint constraints alongside maintained output quality. There is an open problem in accurately predicting and optimizing the complex trade-offs among energy, latency, and response quality to enable deployment of cost-effective, high-performing LLMs on edge or constrained GPU accelerators.

APPROACH:  
We propose to construct an empirical scaling law model that maps LLM scale variables (model size, layer count, precision) and inference settings (batch size, sequence length) to a joint energy-latency-quality landscape by combining: (1) fine-grained transient hardware power measurements on diverse edge GPU accelerators; (2) real-time latency distributions measured across interactive dialogue benchmarks; and (3) quality metrics derived from downstream task evaluations. This model will be learned via inverse problem techniques that efficiently fit multi-objective scaling surfaces, allowing rapid identification of Pareto-optimal scaling points that respect strict energy (<10W average), latency (<100ms 99th percentile), and quality (within 2% BLEU/F1 degradation) constraints. The framework will leverage multi-fidelity data collection and transfer learning across hardware platforms to generalize predictions to unseen devices and usage scenarios.

EXPERIMENT:  
Validate on multiple interactive dialogue and reasoning benchmarks, including DSTC10, SQuAD 2.0, and GLUE/SuperGLUE, using baseline models GPT-3 (175B), LLaMA-2 (13B), and Falcon-40B. Evaluation metrics include 99th percentile latency below 100ms, average energy consumption reduction of at least 20% relative to baseline configurations, and performance retention within 2% absolute of baseline BLEU, F1, or accuracy scores. Experiments will be conducted on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier and A100 GPU edge accelerators under fixed compute budgets (~100 TFLOPs) with at least 5 random seeds per setting. Ablation studies will isolate the impact of transient power profiling, multi-task quality modeling, and inverse problem fitting.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that optimizes energy or latency separately or solely focuses on training compute scaling laws, this approach explicitly models the complex, transient joint energy-latency-quality trade-offs during inference and enables rapid, data-efficient identification of deployment-optimal LLM scaling points under strict real-time constraints. It leverages inverse problem formulations to discover multi-objective scaling laws that prior methods cannot produce, thereby directly guiding the design and deployment of interactive LLMs for edge environments with predictable and balanced resource usage.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a unified adaptive scaling framework for Large Language Models that dynamically modulates model size and subcomponent activation based on real-time joint battery and memory constraints on mobile devices, optimizing a combined metric of energy efficiency and memory footprint while preserving downstream task accuracy.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for Large Language Models (LLMs) primarily focus on model size, data, or compute trade-offs without integrating hardware resource constraints such as battery life and memory simultaneously. Existing adaptive scaling approaches either optimize memory usage or energy consumption independently, resulting in suboptimal performance, application crashes, or rapid battery drain on smartphones and IoT devices. The open problem is to establish a principled, real-time scaling law and control strategy that jointly accounts for both battery state and memory availability to enable sustainable, efficient LLM deployment on mobile platforms.

APPROACH:  
We propose Memory-Constrained Adaptive Model Scaling with Battery Awareness (MACS-BA), a controller that continuously monitors device battery level and available memory and selectively activates or deactivates LLM submodules (e.g., transformer layers, attention heads) according to a learned policy governed by a multi-objective optimization. The method incorporates power profiling data into the scaling decision process, employing a lightweight reinforcement learning agent trained on paired battery-memory states and accuracy feedback from downstream tasks. This approach yields a scaling law that directly maps battery-memory states to optimal model configurations, balancing inference accuracy, memory footprint, and battery drain in real-time.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate MACS-BA on smartphone platforms (e.g., Snapdragon-based devices) using the SQuAD 2.0, GLUE, and Stanford Sentiment Treebank (SST-2) datasets. Metrics include peak memory usage reduction (target >25% vs baseline), battery discharge rate decrease (target >20% improvement in energy efficiency), and maintaining task accuracy above 90% relative to full model baselines. Compare against fixed-size LLMs such as BERT-base, MobileBERT, and adaptive scaling baselines like RAPL-based dynamic scaling and energy-aware pruning. Conduct ablation studies over battery thresholds and memory budgets, and run each experiment with 5 seeds to report mean and variance.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that derives scaling laws solely based on compute or memory (e.g., inverse problem-based scaling) or optimizes energy or memory independently, MACS-BA explicitly integrates real-time battery state as a first-class constraint alongside memory into a novel adaptive scaling law. This joint optimization and fine-grained subcomponent activation strategy enables provably better trade-offs, avoiding both battery exhaustion and memory overflows, which prior SOTA methods cannot balance simultaneously.

topic: Scaling laws for Large Language Models
text:
IDEA:  
Develop and empirically validate dynamic scaling laws for conversational AI models that incorporate continuous adaptation mechanisms to handle rapidly evolving language and slang on social media, optimizing model size, training data update frequency, and filtering strategies for sustained relevance and efficiency.

BACKGROUND:  
Current scaling laws for large language models (LLMs) do not account for the unique challenges posed by conversational AI on social media, where language evolves rapidly due to new slang, memes, and shifting context. This results in model degradation over time because static models either become outdated or require costly retraining with excessively large datasets, leading to capacity bottlenecks and data drift. The open problem is establishing principled scaling laws that incorporate continuous data updates and filtering to maintain performance without linearly increasing model or compute resources.

APPROACH:  
We propose a framework to derive scaling laws that explicitly model the trade-offs between model size, the rate of data update (i.e., frequency and amount of new data incorporated), and the effectiveness of linguistic trend filtering mechanisms. This involves (1) designing an adaptive training loop that incrementally retrains or fine-tunes conversational LLMs with filtered, temporally segmented social media data streams (e.g., Twitter and Reddit), (2) measuring how these factors affect perplexity, downstream dialogue coherence, and robustness to novel slang, and (3) fitting empirical scaling laws that predict performance decay and recovery as functions of update frequency, dataset size, and model capacity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Experiments will use publicly available social media dialogue datasets including Twitter Conversational Corpus, Reddit Conversations Dataset, and the DSTC9-Interactive Dialogue dataset for evaluation. Baselines include GPT-4, DialoGPT, and BlenderBot 3. Metrics are perplexity (target <15 on recent data), F1 score for dialogue relevance (>3% absolute gain over GPT-4 fine-tuned baseline), and slang adaptation accuracy (>5% gain over DialoGPT). The experimental protocol will run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a 2-month compute budget, including 5 random seeds per setting and ablations on update frequency, filtering quality, and model sizes ranging from 125M to 6B parameters.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that treats scaling laws as static relationships with fixed datasets and architectures, this research introduces dynamic scaling laws that integrate temporal data shifts and continuous learning strategies explicitly for conversational AI on social media. This enables precise quantification and optimization of update schedules and filtering methods—capabilities absent from previously established scaling laws that focus primarily on static pretraining regimes or static downstream tasks.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Develop a memristive in-memory neuromorphic processor architecture with adaptive low-voltage operation and dynamic workload-aware control, explicitly mitigating device aging and environmental variability to enable robust, energy-efficient mobile neuromorphic computing under intermittent workload spikes.

BACKGROUND: Mobile neuromorphic processors require stringent energy efficiency and robustness due to limited battery life, thermal constraints, and variable operating conditions, yet existing memristive in-memory computing solutions fail to support low-voltage operation and lack mechanisms to handle intermittent workload surges typical of mobile environments. Moreover, environmental variability and device aging degrade memristive device reliability and accuracy over time, limiting the practical deployment of such systems in real-world mobile applications.

APPROACH: We propose a memristive crossbar architecture optimized for ultra-low voltage operation combined with an adaptive control scheme that dynamically allocates computational resources in response to workload fluctuations detected via real-time monitoring. The system integrates device-aging-aware calibration algorithms and environment-sensing feedback loops to compensate for drift and variability in memristive device characteristics. This includes lightweight on-chip aging diagnostics and temperature-aware voltage scaling to maintain accuracy and energy efficiency. The architecture supports workload burst tolerance by temporarily increasing voltage and parallelism under strict energy budgets.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on neuromorphic benchmarks including N-MNIST, DVS Gesture Dataset, and ImageNet-1K for spiking and traditional neuromorphic workloads. Baselines for comparison include Memristive In-Memory Computing (MIMC) architectures from prior works such as Lee et al. (2020), the Hardware-Implemented DropConnect transistor (Zhang et al., 2025), and the Vertical Dual-Gate Neurotransistor platform (Kim et al., 2023). Metrics will assess: (1) energy consumption per inference aiming for >30% reduction compared to Lee et al., (2) robustness under temperature variation quantified by <5% accuracy degradation versus baseline’s >15%, and (3) sustained accuracy after simulated aging for 10⁴ write cycles with <3% drop. Experiments will run on FPGA-accelerated memristor emulators, with five random seeds and ablation studies isolating aging compensation and workload adaptation effects.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior memristive neuromorphic designs that do not address low-voltage operation under intermittent mobile workloads or lack integrated aging and environmental compensation, this work uniquely combines adaptive workload-aware voltage control with on-chip aging diagnostics to maintain energy efficiency and reliability in dynamic, resource-constrained mobile scenarios.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a dynamically adaptive spike communication and processing architecture for neuromorphic sensor fusion and control in battery-powered autonomous drones that minimizes energy consumption while maintaining ultra-low latency under variable environmental conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current neuromorphic methods for autonomous drones fail to efficiently adapt sparse spike-based communication to rapidly changing real-world sensory inputs and latency-critical control feedback loops, resulting in excessive energy use or delayed responses. This bottleneck limits drone flight autonomy and operational duration, especially in unpredictable, dynamic environments where fast sensor fusion and control decisions are essential. The open problem is how to optimize spike communication protocols and neuromorphic processing to achieve strict energy and latency targets simultaneously in drones.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical, event-driven spike communication protocol that dynamically adjusts spike transmission thresholds and compression ratios based on environmental input variability and control urgency, integrated with a neuromorphic processor architecture that allocates compute resources adaptively across sensor fusion and control modules. This approach incorporates online learning to predict spike sparsity patterns and employs low-power configurable synaptic devices for multilevel spike-weight modulation, enabling real-time optimization of energy-latency tradeoffs. The system includes a hardware-in-the-loop testbed with battery-powered drone platforms to validate energy savings without compromising control responsiveness.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on neuromorphic sensory data from the DVS Gesture Dataset, the N-MNIST dataset for event-based vision, and the UAV123 dataset for real-world drone flight scenarios. Compare against baselines including TrueNorth, Loihi 2, and IBM’s DYNAP-SE neuromorphic chips. Metrics include reduction in energy consumption per inference (>30% reduction vs. Loihi 2), latency under 10 ms for sensor fusion and control loop closure (30% lower than TrueNorth), and sustained autonomous flight duration increase (>20% improvement). Experiments will run with a 100-hour compute budget on FPGA-accelerated neuromorphic hardware, employing 5 random seeds and comprehensive ablation on threshold adaptation and resource allocation components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior hardware-focused or static spike coding schemes (e.g., multibit optoelectronic synapses or hardware DropConnect implementations), this work introduces real-time adaptive spike communication and processing tailored for latency-critical, energy-constrained drone autonomy. Prior work does not address dynamic environmental adaptation of spike sparsity and resource allocation to simultaneously optimize energy and latency in live autonomous flight control loops.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop ultra-low-power, event-driven spike encoding and decoding circuits based on asynchronous mixed-signal neuromorphic hardware optimized for sparse sensor data streams in edge IoT devices, achieving real-time processing with >40% reduction in energy consumption and >15% accuracy improvement over state-of-the-art neuromorphic systems.

BACKGROUND:  
Current neuromorphic approaches for edge IoT devices struggle to balance energy efficiency and computational accuracy when processing sparse, intermittent sensor data under tight power and area constraints. Existing spike encoding/decoding hardware often incurs high latency or excessive energy consumption, limiting responsiveness and scalability in real-world event-driven IoT scenarios.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel architecture combining asynchronous mixed-signal spike encoding circuits with adaptive, event-driven decoding modules that dynamically adjust precision based on input sparsity and temporal patterns. Key components include a low-overhead analog front-end for spike detection, a configurable lightweight digital accumulator for spike decoding, and power gating mechanisms triggered by activity detection. This hardware-software co-design leverages sparse coding principles and asynchronous handshake protocols to minimize idle power and enable real-time, low-latency inference on streaming sensor data.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on neuromorphic sensor datasets including N-MNIST, DVS Gesture, and CIFAR10-DVS for event-based vision tasks, comparing against neuromorphic baselines such as TrueNorth, Loihi 2, and Intel’s Dynap-SE1. Metrics include spike encoding/decoding energy per inference (target <10 nJ), classification accuracy (target >75% on DVS Gesture), and end-to-end latency (target <10 ms), with ablation studies on power gating and adaptive decoding. Experiments will run on FPGA prototyping platforms and custom ASIC simulations, leveraging 5 independent seeds and a 100,000 event sample budget for statistical significance.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works which focus primarily on synaptic device improvements or rely on synchronous architectures, our solution uniquely integrates asynchronous mixed-signal encoding/decoding optimized for sparse, event-driven data, enabling substantial energy savings without sacrificing accuracy or latency. This approach overcomes the scalability and responsiveness limitations of existing neuromorphic hardware in resource-constrained edge IoT applications.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA: Integrate ultra-fine-grained dynamic power gating with hierarchical sparse memory allocation and event-driven synaptic pruning in asynchronous spiking neural networks to simultaneously reduce idle power and memory footprint by over 40% without accuracy loss.

BACKGROUND: Current asynchronous spiking neural networks maintain static memory reservations and coarse-grained power gating, leading to unnecessary energy consumption and memory use during sparse event activity. Prior work reduces idle power by dynamically powering down inactive regions but cannot fully exploit hierarchical event sparsity or adapt memory dynamically at multiple granularities. Deep integration of synaptic pruning during runtime to reduce both memory and power remains an open challenge in event-driven neuromorphic architectures.

APPROACH: We propose a hierarchical memory and power management architecture for asynchronous spiking neural networks that partitions memory and circuitry into multi-scale blocks (e.g., neuron groups, synapse clusters) which are dynamically allocated and powered based on real-time event sparsity statistics. An adaptive event-driven synaptic pruning mechanism selectively disables low-activity synapses to further reduce memory usage and idle power. Fine-grained power gating circuits rapidly power down inactive blocks at multiple hierarchy levels, minimizing leakage power. The system employs sparse event encoding combined with dynamic memory compaction to continuously optimize resource utilization during inference on neuromorphic processors.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on DVS Gesture, N-MNIST, and CIFAR10-DVS datasets measuring total system memory usage, idle and active power consumption, and classification accuracy. Baselines include the winning method "Memory-Constrained Event-Driven Asynchronous Spiking Networks with Minimal Idle Power," the convolutional neuromorphic network approach (2016), and a recent hardware-implemented DropConnect spiking network. Metrics target >40% reductions in idle power and memory use versus baselines without more than 0.5% accuracy degradation. Experiments will run on custom FPGA and neuromorphic ASIC prototypes with a fixed training budget of 100 epochs, 5 random seeds, and comprehensive ablation studies on pruning thresholds and power gating granularity.

NOVELTY: Unlike existing systems that statically allocate memory or only perform coarse power gating, our method introduces a hierarchical, dynamic memory and power adaptation combined with live synaptic pruning tuned by event activity statistics, enabling significantly greater energy and memory efficiency unattainable by prior asynchronous neuromorphic architectures or optoelectronic synapse hardware approaches.

topic: Energy-efficient neuromorphic computing
text:
IDEA:  
Develop an adaptive spike-threshold neuromorphic architecture with dynamic event-routing and energy-aware on-chip learning that minimizes energy per spike while ensuring low-latency responsiveness under variable input conditions for battery-powered edge devices.

BACKGROUND:  
Current neuromorphic approaches for edge devices suffer from inefficient energy-latency trade-offs due to static spike thresholding and fixed event routing, which lead to unnecessary spike generation or processing delays under unpredictable and sparse input activity. These inefficiencies reduce battery life and limit real-world applicability in power-constrained environments such as wearable sensors and autonomous drones. There is a critical need for mechanisms that dynamically adapt computation to input sparsity and temporal variability to optimize energy consumption without sacrificing responsiveness.

APPROACH:  
We propose a neuromorphic system integrating (1) adaptive spike threshold modulation based on local and global activity statistics to precisely control neuron firing rates and avoid redundant spikes, (2) dynamic event routing that reconfigures spike propagation paths in real time to prioritize latency-critical signals and minimize routing overhead, and (3) an energy-aware on-chip learning algorithm that adjusts synaptic weights and thresholds to balance accuracy and power under shifting workload conditions. The architecture will leverage hardware-friendly lightweight statistics modules for threshold adaptation and a reconfigurable event bus for routing, all optimized for ultra-low-power CMOS neuromorphic cores.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on neuromorphic datasets including N-MNIST, DVS Gesture Dataset, and CIFAR10-DVS for event-based vision, comparing against baselines Spiking ResNet-18, Loihi 2 neuromorphic processor, and TrueNorth. Metrics include >15% reduction in average energy per spike, >20% reduction in end-to-end latency, and maintaining classification accuracy within 1% of baseline. Ablations will measure impact of adaptive thresholding and dynamic routing separately. Experiments will use FPGA prototyping with power measurement tools, training over 5 seeds and fixed compute budgets equivalent to 100 GPU-hours.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that employs static spike thresholds or hardware-implemented stochastic functions without dynamic adaptation (e.g., ferroelectric synaptic DropConnect) or only improves synapse device characteristics, this method jointly optimizes spike generation and event propagation dynamically under variable input patterns, achieving a previously unattainable energy-latency balance critical for real-world edge neuromorphic applications.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Develop a lightweight, physics-informed generative AI framework for real-time molecular design that runs efficiently on edge devices with strict computational and power constraints, enabling on-site drug discovery and molecular optimization without reliance on centralized cloud infrastructure.

BACKGROUND: Current generative AI models for drug discovery require extensive computational resources and high-latency cloud or HPC environments, preventing their deployment in resource-constrained or decentralized settings such as point-of-care clinics or remote field laboratories. This bottleneck limits rapid, on-demand molecular design and decision-making in scenarios where immediate therapeutic candidate generation is critical. There is a clear need for a physics-aware generative modeling approach optimized for edge devices that preserves simulation fidelity while meeting strict latency and memory constraints.

APPROACH: We propose a novel generative framework that integrates physics-informed neural networks with lightweight molecular graph generative architectures, optimized via model pruning, quantization, and knowledge distillation for deployment on ARM-based edge hardware. The method incorporates differentiable molecular dynamics-inspired constraints to ensure chemical validity and binding affinity approximations in real-time. Our architecture fuses a compact graph-based VAE backbone with a lightweight transformer module for goal-directed molecular optimization, enabling inference latency below 100ms on devices such as NVIDIA Jetson Nano or Raspberry Pi 4. Training will leverage multi-fidelity datasets combining QM9 quantum chemical data and ChEMBL bioactivity annotations to balance accuracy and efficiency.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate on benchmark datasets QM9, ChEMBL 27, and the MOSES molecular generation dataset, comparing against baselines including ChemVAE, GraphAF, and MolGPT. Metrics will include validity (>98%), novelty (>90%), and drug-likeness (QED score >0.6), plus inference latency reduction by at least 50% compared to ChemVAE on edge hardware, and memory footprint under 512MB. Experimental protocol includes training on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs for up to 72 hours with 5 random seeds to ensure statistical significance, followed by deployment tests on Jetson Nano and Raspberry Pi 4. Ablations will isolate the impact of physics constraints and model compression strategies.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work which either focuses on high-performance centralized generative AI (e.g., GPT-2/LSTM for molecular design) or physics-based simulations lacking edge optimization, this approach uniquely combines physics-informed generative modeling with aggressive edge device optimization. It delivers real-time, on-device molecular generation with competitive chemical validity and affinity approximations, enabling practical decentralized drug discovery inaccessible to existing frameworks.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
A reinforcement learning framework with an adaptive multi-objective reward balancing mechanism that jointly optimizes therapeutic target affinity, comprehensive ADMET profiles, and scaffold diversity to generate novel, drug-like molecules with robust real-world applicability.

BACKGROUND:  
Current RL-based molecular generators frequently optimize single objectives or apply static reward heuristics, often overlooking critical ADMET failure modes and scaffold diversity, resulting in molecules with poor clinical translation potential. Existing multi-objective approaches struggle to maintain optimal trade-offs, often sacrificing novelty or stability for efficacy or vice versa. There remains an unmet need for principled, dynamic reward balancing that ensures simultaneous optimization of therapeutic activity, ADMET safety, and molecular novelty.

APPROACH:  
We propose a reinforcement learning molecular generator utilizing a dynamically adjusted, multi-objective reward function that balances binding affinity (modeled via docking scores or predicted IC50), ADMET properties (toxicity, solubility, metabolic stability), and scaffold diversity (measured by Bemis-Murcko scaffold uniqueness). The reward balancing algorithm employs a Pareto-front-guided adaptive weighting scheme updated per training epoch to avoid dominance by any single objective. Molecular candidates are encoded with a graph-based neural policy network trained on BindingDB for affinity and Tox21 plus ADMET benchmark datasets for safety profiles. Novelty and diversity constraints are enforced through scaffold-based penalties and data-driven stability predictors to maintain chemical validity and structural diversity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will use BindingDB for binding affinity, Tox21 and ADMET Benchmark datasets (e.g., ADMETlab, ChEMBL ADMET annotations) for toxicity and pharmacokinetics, and ZINC15 for assessing scaffold diversity and novelty metrics. Baselines include MolDQN, REINVENT 2.0, and the original multi-objective reward balancing RL model. Metrics will measure >5% absolute improvement in multi-objective Pareto front hypervolume, >10% increase in unique Bemis-Murcko scaffolds over baselines, and at least 15% reduction in predicted ADMET failure rates. Training runs will be performed on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a 48-hour budget per model, averaging results over 5 random seeds with ablation studies on reward components and adaptive weighting mechanisms.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods that use fixed or heuristic reward shaping, our approach introduces a principled, adaptive multi-objective Pareto front balancing that explicitly incorporates scaffold diversity alongside binding affinity and ADMET, ensuring no objective is sacrificed. This leads to chemically novel, stable molecules with balanced therapeutic and safety profiles unattainable by existing RL molecular generators.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a latency-constrained, multi-objective reinforcement learning framework for real-time on-device molecular generation that integrates dynamic quantization-aware policy optimization and adaptive reward shaping to enable sub-100ms inference on ARM Cortex-M-class devices while optimizing drug-like property trade-offs.

BACKGROUND:  
Current state-of-the-art molecular generative models achieve high accuracy but suffer from prohibitive inference latency, preventing deployment on resource-limited edge devices needed for rapid experimental feedback in decentralized or field drug discovery settings. Existing latency-constrained reinforcement learning approaches, such as "Latency-Constrained Reinforcement Learning for On-Device Molecular Generation," optimize for single-objective metrics with static reward approximations and fixed quantization schemes, limiting adaptability and multi-property optimization under strict latency budgets.

APPROACH:  
We propose a lightweight reinforcement learning agent that employs dynamic quantization-aware training with mixed-precision neural networks, combined with an adaptive reward shaping mechanism that prioritizes multiple molecular objectives (e.g., topological similarity, synthetic accessibility, drug-likeness). The method incorporates a latency predictor integrated into the policy gradient update to enforce hard sub-100ms inference constraints on ARM Cortex-M55 processors. The policy network architecture is designed with modular, latency-aware blocks allowing runtime adjustment of precision and computational pathways based on observed hardware performance and molecular complexity, enabling real-time multi-objective molecular optimization under strict latency constraints.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on MOSES, ZINC-250k, and GuacaMol benchmark datasets, measuring topological similarity (Tanimoto similarity), synthetic accessibility score (SAS), and Quantitative Estimate of Drug-likeness (QED). Baselines include Latency-Constrained Reinforcement Learning for On-Device Molecular Generation (the tournament winner), MolDQN, and GraphAF. Metrics will quantify sub-100ms inference latency on ARM Cortex-M55 hardware, multi-objective reward convergence, and molecular validity with expected improvements of >20% in multi-objective optimization scores at equal or lower latency compared to baselines. Experiments will be run with at least 5 seeds, including ablations for dynamic quantization and adaptive reward shaping components, under a compute budget of 72 GPU-hours on NVIDIA A100s for offline training and ARM Cortex-M real-device profiling.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, this method explicitly integrates dynamic quantization and latency prediction into the reinforcement learning update loop to adaptively balance multi-objective molecular optimization with hard latency constraints on edge devices, enabling real-time, on-device deployment with finer-grained trade-offs that prior static-quantized, single-objective models cannot achieve.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable, cloud-native reinforcement learning framework for ultra-high-throughput virtual screening that efficiently handles delayed and batched reward signals to enable generation and prioritization of millions of drug-like molecules in parallel at industrial scale.

BACKGROUND:  
Current generative AI methods for drug discovery, including reinforcement learning (RL)-based molecular generation, struggle to scale to industrial-scale virtual screening of millions of candidates due to inefficient parallelization and inability to handle delayed or batched reward feedback from expensive molecular evaluation pipelines. This bottleneck prevents practical application of RL for large-scale molecular generation and optimization in real-world drug discovery.

APPROACH:  
We propose a RL framework explicitly designed for cloud-scale deployment, featuring (1) asynchronous, batched policy updates to accommodate delayed feedback from computationally expensive docking and bioactivity simulations, (2) distributed actor-learner architecture optimized for GPU/TPU clusters enabling parallel generation of molecular candidates, and (3) reward shaping techniques and curriculum learning to accelerate convergence despite sparse and delayed molecular evaluation signals. The molecular generator is based on a graph-based generative model augmented with RL to optimize binding affinity and drug-likeness simultaneously.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on benchmark molecular datasets including ZINC-250K, ChEMBL (release 29), and MOSES for generative quality and drug-likeness; use GLORYS and DUD-E for docking-based virtual screening evaluation. Compare against baselines such as REINVENT 2.0, MolDQN, and GraphAF. Metrics include: (1) success rate of generating molecules surpassing affinity thresholds (>15% improvement over REINVENT on docking scores), (2) scalability measured by throughput (molecules screened per hour per 100 GPUs, targeting >3x improvement), and (3) sample efficiency with fewer policy updates to reach target molecular quality. Experiments run on a cloud cluster with 128 GPUs over 3 random seeds, including ablation studies on reward delay tolerance and batch size.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior generative AI and RL approaches that operate on small-to-moderate molecular sets with near-immediate reward feedback, this work solves the open problem of scalability and delayed reward handling by integrating asynchronous distributed RL with batched molecular evaluations, enabling industrial-scale virtual screening workflows that were previously infeasible.

topic: Generative AI for drug discovery and molecular design
text:
IDEA: Develop a lightweight physics-informed generative AI framework that enables physically valid molecular design and optimization in low-resource experimental labs by integrating simplified physics constraints directly into transformer-based generative models.

BACKGROUND: Many advanced generative AI approaches for drug discovery require extensive computational power and large datasets to ensure physically meaningful molecular generation, making them inaccessible to low-resource experimental labs. Existing physics-based molecular design methods demand high-throughput screening and compute budgets that small labs cannot afford, resulting in a critical bottleneck for iterative drug discovery cycles with physical validity guarantees under resource constraints. Bridging this gap requires models that maintain physical fidelity while operating efficiently on limited compute and data.

APPROACH: We propose a novel transformer-based generative model architecture augmented with lightweight physics-informed inductive biases—such as simplified quantum-chemical constraints, approximate energy-based regularizers, and physics-driven latent space shaping—that drastically reduce the need for high-throughput data and compute. This model is trained with multi-task objectives combining molecular validity, approximate physical property prediction, and generative diversity, enabling iterative molecular optimization using only modest GPU resources typical in small labs. The approach leverages benchmark datasets alongside domain-specific low-data augmentation to ensure broad applicability and robustness in low-resource settings.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the QM9, MoleculeNet (specifically the ESOL and FreeSolv subsets), and ChEMBL v27 datasets, comparing against baselines including GraphVAE, ChemBERTa, and Physics-Informed Neural Networks (PINNs) adapted for molecular generation. Metrics include molecular validity (>98%), novelty (>85%), synthetic accessibility (SAS score improvement by >10%), predicted binding affinity regression error (RMSE reduced by >15% vs baselines), and compute efficiency (training time reduced by >50% on a single NVIDIA RTX 3060 GPU). Experiments include five random seeds, ablations on physics constraints, and evaluations under small data regimes (≤10K training samples).

NOVELTY: Unlike prior physics-based molecular generative models that rely on large-scale compute or data, or purely data-driven transformers that ignore physical validity under resource constraints, this work uniquely integrates simplified physics-informed constraints into lightweight generative transformers optimized specifically for low-resource setups. This enables physically valid molecular design without the need for extensive high-throughput data or HPC infrastructure, democratizing advanced drug discovery capabilities to under-resourced experimental laboratories.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Integrate probabilistic logical constraints derived from ecological expert knowledge into a Bayesian causal discovery framework to improve stability and accuracy of causal graph pruning under severe data sparsity and missingness in ecological networks.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery methods for ecological systems often fail due to sample scarcity and missing data, producing unstable causal graphs that hinder reliable ecological inference. While Logical Constraint Integration (LCI) has demonstrated improvements by embedding deterministic hard and soft constraints into graph pruning, it cannot quantify uncertainty introduced by noisy or partially conflicting domain knowledge, limiting robustness when expert knowledge contradicts sparse observational data. Addressing this requires a framework that fuses expert knowledge probabilistically with data-driven discovery to maintain graph stability and ecological validity under uncertainty.

APPROACH:  
We propose Probabilistic Logical Constraint Integration (PLCI), a novel causal discovery method that encodes expert knowledge as probabilistic logical constraints within a Bayesian causal structure learning framework. PLCI models constraints as priors with uncertainty estimates, allowing soft enforcement rather than hard pruning, and employs Markov Chain Monte Carlo (MCMC) sampling to jointly infer causal graphs and constraint adherence under data sparsity and missingness. The method adapts existing constraint-based pruning (e.g., PC algorithm) by weighting conditional independence tests with constraint-derived probabilities, stabilizing causal edge selection. Validation includes a specialized inference module that compares inferred causal directions with expert-annotated ecological interactions to assess ecological plausibility.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate PLCI on three real-world ecological longitudinal datasets: (1) the Moorea Coral Reef Long Term Ecological Research (LTER) dataset; (2) the Global Biodiversity Information Facility (GBIF) species interaction data subset; and (3) the North Temperate Lakes Long-Term Ecological Research (NTL-LTER) food web data. Baselines include Logical Constraint Integration (LCI), KiNCD, and S-FCI. Metrics: causal graph stability measured by Jaccard similarity (>5% absolute improvement over LCI), edge direction accuracy (>3% improvement vs KiNCD), and ecological validity assessed by agreement with expert annotations (>10% increase). Experimental protocol involves 5 random seeds for robustness, ablation on constraint confidence weighting, and computational budget of 48 hours on NVIDIA A100 GPUs.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike LCI’s deterministic constraints, PLCI uniquely integrates expert knowledge probabilistically within a Bayesian causal discovery paradigm, enabling principled uncertainty quantification and robustness to noisy or conflicting expert inputs. Prior methods like KiNCD and S-FCI cannot incorporate domain constraints probabilistically, nor do they explicitly address data sparsity combined with logical expert knowledge, making PLCI a first to stabilize ecological causal discovery through probabilistic logic-driven regularization.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a unified adaptive nonlinear causal discovery framework that integrates online latent confounder modeling, regime-aware continuous optimization, and multiphase intervention inference to robustly recover causal graphs from nonstationary, high-dimensional observational financial time series.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing causal discovery methods, including the winning Adaptive Constraint-Based Causal Discovery for Nonstationary Financial Time Series, assume stationarity or handle regime shifts only at a surface level, leading to inaccurate causal inferences under rapid market dynamics and latent confounding. Current nonlinear causal discovery approaches like KiNCD lack adaptive mechanisms for nonstationarity or explicit latent confounder tracking in evolving environments. The open problem is to simultaneously handle nonlinear dependencies, latent confounders, and regime shifts in real-time causal graph recovery from observational financial data, enabling reliable downstream market predictions and risk management.

APPROACH:  
We propose Adaptive Regime-Aware Nonlinear Causal Optimization with Latent Confounder Tracking (ARNO-LCT), which combines: (1) an online change-point detection module integrated with a latent variable augmented Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) to model nonlinear dependencies and dynamically adjust to regime shifts; (2) a continuous optimization-based causal graph search that incorporates latent confounder constraints learned from interventional proxies extracted from natural financial perturbations; and (3) a multiphase intervention inference step leveraging detected regime boundaries as pseudo-interventions to refine causal directionality. This approach iteratively updates the causal graph and latent variable estimates to maintain accuracy amid evolving market conditions.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate ARNO-LCT on the Tick-by-Tick FX dataset, NYSE high-frequency equity trades dataset, and the LOBSTER Limit Order Book dataset, comparing against Adaptive Constraint-Based Causal Discovery, KiNCD, and S-FCI baselines. Metrics include causal graph stability across regimes (measured by Structural Hamming Distance reduction >15%), predictive accuracy of directional market movement (>5% uplift in directional F1-score), and latent confounder identification accuracy (measured by average precision >0.7). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs over 5 random seeds with ablations on latent confounder modeling and regime adaptation components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, ARNO-LCT uniquely integrates nonlinear continuous optimization with explicit latent confounder tracking and uses regime boundaries as natural interventions for refined causal inference in nonstationary financial time series.
