This enables robust causal discovery under rapid, high-dimensional regime shifts that neither existing constraint-based adaptive methods nor nonlinear causal discovery frameworks with static assumptions can reliably achieve.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a scalable, privacy-preserving deep generative causal discovery framework that explicitly models heterogeneous batch effects and missingness in large-scale healthcare records to robustly recover disentangled causal structures without interventional data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery methods fail to reliably uncover stable causal relationships in large, high-dimensional healthcare datasets due to pervasive missing data, patient heterogeneity, and confounding batch effects introduced by diverse data collection sites. These challenges hinder clinical adoption by producing unstable causal graphs that do not generalize across populations or respect patient privacy constraints. Existing approaches like KiNCD or S-FCI either lack scalability to healthcare-sized datasets or do not explicitly address missingness and batch confounding simultaneously in a generative modeling framework.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel deep generative model integrating variational inference with disentangled representation learning that explicitly parameterizes batch effects and latent confounders via structured latent variables. The model incorporates healthcare domain knowledge as priors on causal graph skeletons to regularize learning. Missing data is handled naturally through the generative process without imputation. Patient privacy is enforced through differential privacy mechanisms integrated into the training loop. The model is trained end-to-end on large-scale observational healthcare datasets to jointly learn causal structure and latent confounders, enabling stable and interpretable causal discovery across heterogeneous populations.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on large-scale electronic health record datasets: MIMIC-IV (critical care data), UK Biobank (population health), and eICU Collaborative Research Database. Baselines include KiNCD, S-FCI, and Orthogonal Structure Search. Metrics: Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) for graph accuracy (aim for >5% absolute improvement over baselines), Precision and Recall of discovered edges (>3% improvement), and robustness measured by stability across patient subgroups (measured by graph similarity >10% better than baselines). Perform ablations on batch effect modeling, missing data handling, and privacy components. Use 4 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with training budgets capped at 72 hours per dataset and 5 random seeds per experiment.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and S-FCI, our method simultaneously models missingness, batch effects, and latent confounders within a deep generative framework tailored for large-scale healthcare data without requiring interventional data. Prior methods cannot scale to datasets with high missingness and heterogeneity nor enforce privacy guarantees, whereas our approach integrates these components holistically and provides stable causal structure recovery validated on multiple real-world clinical datasets.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a lightweight, streaming-capable causal discovery framework that incrementally updates nonlinear causal graphs directly on edge devices under severe computational and memory constraints, with robust noise and concept drift adaptation for real-time sensor data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current causal discovery methods, including KiNCD and S-FCI, achieve strong accuracy or theoretical guarantees but rely on batch processing and substantial computational resources, making them unsuitable for continuous, online adaptation on edge devices with limited compute and memory. Real-time IoT sensor data streams exacerbate challenges due to noise, nonstationarity, and lack of interventional data, causing existing methods to degrade in performance without expensive retraining. There is an unmet need for causal discovery techniques that efficiently maintain and update causal representations on-device in a streaming manner, robust to concept drift and noise.

APPROACH:  
We propose LightStream-CD, a novel incremental causal discovery algorithm leveraging a compressed Kolmogorov-Arnold Network (KAN) latent representation combined with an adaptive Bayesian online update scheme for nonlinear causal graphs. The method uses a memory- and compute-efficient parametric form for causal mechanisms allowing constant update complexity per new sample, integrates drift detection to trigger lightweight local structure refinement, and employs noise-robust inference via online variational Bayesian filtering. The architecture runs on-device using quantized model components and sparse graph updates to meet edge constraints without batch retraining.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate LightStream-CD on real-world streaming sensor datasets: (1) Intel Lab Data (indoor environmental sensor network), (2) Numenta Anomaly Benchmark (NAB) with multivariate sensor streams, and (3) TUM Kitchen Dataset (multi-sensor activity recognition). Baselines include KiNCD, S-FCI, and Orthogonal Structure Search (OSS). Metrics: (a) Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) with >10% absolute improvement over baselines on streaming updates; (b) runtime latency per update <50ms on Raspberry Pi-class edge device; (c) robustness measured by >15% degradation reduction in causal accuracy under injected noise and concept drift scenarios. Protocol: 5 random seeds per dataset, ablations on drift detection and Bayesian update components, training compute limited to <1 GFLOP per update.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike KiNCD and S-FCI, which require batch processing and full retraining, LightStream-CD achieves fully online, incremental causal graph updates with bounded compute and memory, enabling real-time adaptation on resource-constrained edge devices. Prior amortized or LLM-based methods lack noise-robust continual adaptation mechanisms and edge suitability, while our approach uniquely combines latent nonlinear causal modeling with Bayesian online inference and drift-aware structure refinement tailored for streaming IoT data.

topic: Causal discovery from observational data
text:
IDEA: Develop a lightweight, incremental causal discovery framework that performs real-time nonlinear causal inference on streaming observational data directly on resource-constrained edge devices by combining compact causal representations with efficient online optimization and noise-robust scoring metrics.

BACKGROUND: Existing state-of-the-art causal discovery methods like KiNCD and amortized causal discovery models achieve high accuracy and robustness but require significant computational resources, batch retraining, or access to multiple data environments, making them unsuitable for deployment on edge devices with limited CPU, memory, and energy budgets. There is a pressing need for methods that enable continuous, real-time causal structure learning directly on edge devices from streaming, noisy observational data without pre-collected large batches or interventional data.

APPROACH: We propose EdgeCausal, a novel causal discovery system that integrates (1) a compressed causal representation inspired by neural sparse coding to succinctly encode nonlinear dependencies, (2) an incremental online optimization algorithm based on stochastic gradient updates with adaptive memory to update causal graphs dynamically, and (3) a noise-robust causal sufficiency scoring function designed for streaming data scenarios that discounts transient noise spikes and adapts to evolving distributions. This pipeline runs efficiently within kilobyte-scale memory and low FLOP budgets, enabling deployment on microcontrollers and mobile CPUs without batch retraining or cloud reliance.

EXPERIMENT: We will evaluate EdgeCausal on multiple benchmark datasets including Sachs (biological signaling networks), CauseMe (simulated nonlinear causal models), and DREAM4 (gene regulatory networks), comparing against baselines KiNCD, Amortized Causal Discovery (ACD), and S-FCI. Metrics include Structural Hamming Distance (SHD) to ground truth causal graphs (targeting >10% absolute SHD reduction vs. baselines on edge-compatible models), runtime latency per causal update cycle (aiming for <100ms on Raspberry Pi 4), and memory footprint (targeting <50MB peak usage). Experiments will be conducted with 5 random seeds, ablation studies isolating representation and scoring modules, and under varying noise levels to test robustness.

NOVELTY: Unlike KiNCD and amortized methods that require batch processing or high compute, and unlike multi-environment algorithms like S-FCI, EdgeCausal uniquely enables fully incremental, real-time nonlinear causal discovery directly on severely resource-constrained hardware without interventional data or offline retraining, addressing the open problem of streaming causal inference on edge devices with strict computational and memory constraints.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA: Develop an ultra-low latency input perturbation sensitivity analysis framework combining fast adaptive perturbation generation with incremental output variance estimation to enable real-time uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks deployed on autonomous systems.

BACKGROUND: Real-time autonomous systems such as self-driving cars and drones require rapid and accurate uncertainty estimates to ensure safe decision-making in dynamic environments. Existing input perturbation sensitivity analyses, while effective, are computationally expensive and exhibit high latency, making them unsuitable for deployment under stringent real-time constraints. This latency bottleneck compromises system responsiveness and safety by delaying uncertainty information critical for downstream control and planning modules.

APPROACH: We propose a two-fold solution: (1) a novel adaptive perturbation generation technique that uses gradient-informed sparse perturbations focused on the most sensitive input dimensions, reducing the number of perturbations needed for uncertainty estimation; and (2) an incremental variance estimation algorithm that efficiently updates output variance statistics in a streaming fashion without recomputing over the entire perturbation set. This approach leverages hardware-friendly approximations (e.g., low-rank perturbation projections) and is designed for parallel execution on embedded GPUs or specialized accelerators. The framework integrates seamlessly with existing DNN architectures and supports continuous online uncertainty quantification during inference.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on three benchmark datasets relevant to autonomous perception and control: KITTI (autonomous driving visual data), nuScenes (multi-modal driving dataset), and the UCI Gas Sensor Array Drift Dataset (time-series sensor data). Baselines include Monte Carlo Dropout, Deep Ensembles, and the Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC) model (2025). Metrics include uncertainty estimation latency (targeting <10ms per forward pass on embedded GPUs), expected calibration error (ECE) improvements >5% absolute over baselines, and out-of-distribution detection AUC with at least 3% gain. Experiments will run on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier hardware with a compute budget capped to 48 hours, using 5 random seeds per experiment and ablations on perturbation sparsity and incremental update frequency.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior methods such as PSC or active subspace approaches that focus on probabilistic modeling or dimensionality reduction within the network parameter or latent space, our method directly targets the latency bottleneck of input perturbation generation and output variance calculation, enabling uncertainty quantification within strict real-time constraints. This is the first framework to combine adaptive sparse perturbations with incremental variance updates specifically optimized for real-time embedded deployment in autonomous systems.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a resource-aware meta-learning framework that dynamically adapts uncertainty quantification models on edge devices by jointly optimizing for heterogeneous personalized data distributions and strict computational, memory, and latency constraints through a novel modular uncertainty adaptation mechanism.

BACKGROUND:  
Current meta-learning approaches for uncertainty quantification (UQ) on edge devices fail to generalize across heterogeneous and limited personal data distributions while respecting severe resource constraints such as low compute, memory, and latency budgets. This results in either inaccurate uncertainty estimates or models that are too heavy for real-time edge deployment. The open problem is designing meta-learners that efficiently adapt uncertainty models individually per user or environment without sacrificing accuracy or exceeding resource budgets.

APPROACH:  
We propose Modular Resource-Aware Meta-Learning for Uncertainty Adaptation (MRAM-UQ), which decomposes the uncertainty quantification model into lightweight, interchangeable modules representing different uncertainty sources (e.g., epistemic, aleatoric). MRAM-UQ employs a meta-optimizer trained on a diverse set of personalized edge-device scenarios that learns to selectively activate and fine-tune modules on-device using few-shot adaptation with constrained compute and memory budgets. The framework incorporates a resource-aware controller that dynamically prunes and compresses modules based on real-time resource monitoring, ensuring latency and memory limits are met without significant loss in uncertainty calibration or prediction accuracy.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate MRAM-UQ on personalized health monitoring with the PAMAP2 Physical Activity Monitoring dataset and real-world speech recognition personalization using the LibriSpeech and Mozilla Common Voice datasets. Baselines include Meta-SGD, MAML, Monte Carlo Dropout, and Deep Ensembles adapted for edge. Metrics: target >3% absolute improvement in expected calibration error (ECE) over baselines, maintain latency <50ms per inference on ARM Cortex-A72 edge CPUs, and reduce peak memory usage by >40% compared to unpruned meta-learners. Experiments run on ARM-based edge hardware with 5 random seeds; ablation studies test the impact of modular pruning and resource-aware control.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing meta-learning UQ approaches that neither explicitly balance heterogeneous personalization nor enforce strict resource constraints, MRAM-UQ introduces modular uncertainty components dynamically adapted under resource-aware control, enabling rapid, efficient, and accurate uncertainty adaptation tailored to individual edge device constraints and data distributions—a capability absent in prior work such as deterministic skip connections or active subspace methods.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop Lightweight Dynamically Adaptive Unsembling Networks (LDANets) that create user-profile-tailored, resource-frugal ensembles by selectively activating compact subnetworks on mobile devices to achieve improved uncertainty quantification and personalized recommendation accuracy under strict hardware constraints.

BACKGROUND:  
Personalized recommendation systems on mobile devices require reliable uncertainty estimates to improve prediction trustworthiness and user satisfaction. However, current ensemble-based uncertainty quantification methods are computationally and memory-intensive, preventing their effective deployment on resource-limited mobile platforms. The open problem is how to maintain or improve uncertainty quantification quality while dynamically adapting ensemble complexity to individual users’ data and device constraints without exceeding power or memory budgets.

APPROACH:  
We propose LDANets, which leverage a pool of lightweight subnetworks pretrained on diverse user behavioral patterns and dynamically select an adaptive ensemble subset per user profile via a low-overhead gating mechanism. This dynamic selection minimizes inference cost by activating only relevant subnetworks, whose outputs are combined using an uncertainty-calibrated aggregation function designed to preserve predictive uncertainty fidelity. The architecture employs parameter-efficient subnetworks inspired by recent advances in active subspaces and probabilistic skip connections, ensuring smooth uncertainty estimates even with reduced complexity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark personalized recommendation datasets: MovieLens-1M, Amazon Electronics Reviews, and Last.fm 1K User Dataset. Compare against baselines including MC Dropout ensembles, Deep Ensemble (Hendrycks & Gimpel, 2017), and Bayesian Personalized Ranking (BPR) with uncertainty extensions. Metrics include expected calibration error (ECE), negative log-likelihood (NLL), recommendation accuracy (HR@10 and NDCG@10) targeting >3% absolute improvement in ECE and NLL reduction by at least 10%, while reducing inference latency and memory usage by 30% relative to Deep Ensemble. Experiments run on representative mobile hardware simulators (e.g., ARM Cortex-A76 CPUs) with 5 random seeds; conduct ablations on subnetwork pool size and gating complexity.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that either relies on static ensembles ill-suited for constrained devices or low-dimensional active subspaces without user-adaptive dynamic complexity, LDANets explicitly integrate dynamic ensemble adaptation based on user profiles with lightweight subnetworks, enabling high-fidelity uncertainty quantification on-device with substantially reduced resource consumption. This approach bridges the gap unaddressed by PSC, active subspaces, or static ensemble methods in mobile personalized recommendation contexts.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Memory-Efficient Probabilistic Ensemble Distillation with Layer-wise Uncertainty Transfer for Edge-Deployable Deep Neural Networks.

BACKGROUND:  
Current ensemble-based uncertainty quantification methods achieve state-of-the-art calibration and robustness but require large memory footprints, making them impractical for edge or autonomous systems with strict resource constraints. Prior distillation methods compress ensembles but fail to preserve the full predictive variance and uncertainty calibration, especially under dataset shift, limiting their reliability for safety-critical decisions. The open problem is how to distill both accurate predictive means and uncertainty distributions from large ensembles into a single compact model with under 50MB memory, while maintaining ensemble-level uncertainty fidelity.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel layer-wise uncertainty distillation framework that transfers not only the ensemble’s predictive mean but also its inter-layer variance and covariance statistics through probabilistic skip connections inspired by PSC (Probabilistic Skip Connections). This approach captures uncertainty propagation through the network, preserving richer uncertainty information than output-only distillation. The compact student model is trained using an uncertainty-aware loss combining expected calibration error (ECE) minimization, Bhattacharyya coefficient alignment of class-conditional output distributions, and active subspace regularization to emphasize critical parameter directions for uncertainty. This multi-objective distillation ensures the student mimics the ensemble’s uncertainty under in-distribution and shifted data without increasing memory footprint beyond 50MB, targeting autonomous driving tasks and real-time edge inference.

EXPERIMENT:  
Validate on three datasets: nuScenes (autonomous driving LiDAR and camera fusion), CIFAR-100-C (corrupted image classification for dataset shift), and ImageNet-1K (large-scale image classification). Baselines include Deep Ensembles (5 models), MC Dropout, Memory-Efficient Ensemble Distillation (from the tournament winner), and Probabilistic Skip Connection models. Evaluate with metrics: Expected Calibration Error (ECE) aiming for ≤5% margin to ensemble, Negative Log-Likelihood (NLL) improved by >3%, model size under 50MB, and robustness via accuracy drop under corruption ≤10%. Experiments run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds per setup, including ablations on layer-wise uncertainty components and loss terms.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior distillation methods that only transfer output uncertainty or predictive means, this method uniquely distills full layer-wise uncertainty statistics via probabilistic skip connections, preserving uncertainty propagation dynamics and calibration under shift. It also integrates Bhattacharyya coefficient-based loss and active subspace regularization to retain inter-class uncertainty and critical parameter sensitivity, achieving ensemble-level fidelity in a single compact model deployable on edge hardware—a capability unmatched by current PSC or ensemble distillation approaches alone.

topic: Uncertainty quantification in deep neural networks
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a deterministic sensitivity analysis framework combining robust Jacobian estimation and certified uncertainty bounds with real-time guaranteed latency for safety-critical autonomous systems operating under adversarial and noisy sensor inputs.

BACKGROUND:  
Existing uncertainty quantification (UQ) methods for deep neural networks in safety-critical autonomous systems either rely on probabilistic approximations or lack deterministic, certified guarantees of safety margins under stringent real-time latency constraints. This gap risks unreliable system behavior during adversarial conditions and noisy sensor inputs, preventing deployment in environments like autonomous driving and aviation where deterministic safety assurances are mandated.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel framework that (1) performs robust Jacobian estimation through noise-resilient finite-difference and automatic-differentiation hybrid methods to tightly characterize local sensitivity of DNN outputs to input perturbations, (2) derives certified deterministic uncertainty bounds by integrating these Jacobians with adversarial input constraint sets, and (3) employs optimized algorithmic techniques including parallelized low-rank approximations and early stopping criteria to guarantee real-time computational budgets (e.g., sub-50ms latency on embedded GPUs). The framework outputs provably safe uncertainty bounds that directly translate into certified safety margins for autonomous decision modules.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the Berkeley DeepDrive BDD100K dataset and the KITTI Vision Benchmark Suite, focusing on object detection and semantic segmentation under noisy and adversarial perturbations. Compare against baselines including MC Dropout, Deep Ensembles, and the Probabilistic Skip Connections (PSC) method. Metrics:  
- Certified safety margin tightness (measured via worst-case output deviation bounds, target <10% looseness vs baseline)  
- Real-time latency (target <50ms per inference on NVIDIA Jetson AGX Xavier)  
- Robustness under adversarial attacks (improve certified robustness radius by >15%)  
Multiple runs (5 seeds) with ablation studies on Jacobian estimation techniques and computational optimizations will be conducted.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike probabilistic or subspace-based UQ methods (e.g., PSC or active subspaces), our approach produces deterministic, certified uncertainty bounds with explicit real-time latency guarantees, directly addressing the safety certification bottleneck in adversarial and noisy environments—a capability no prior work currently achieves comprehensively.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a hardware-aware neural architecture search (NAS) framework that jointly optimizes deep learning climate emulators for strict low-power embedded deployment constraints while enabling adaptive, explainable model pruning to balance accuracy and resource usage in in-situ sensor networks.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate models are typically designed without explicit integration of embedded hardware constraints such as memory footprint, energy consumption, and inference latency, hindering their deployability in remote, power-limited sensor networks. Existing NAS methods either prioritize predictive accuracy alone or lack fine-grained adaptability post-search, limiting continuous autonomous climate monitoring capabilities under varying operational conditions.

APPROACH: We propose a multi-objective, hardware-aware NAS framework that incorporates real embedded device profiling (e.g., ARM Cortex-M MCUs and Nvidia Jetson Nano) into the search loop, simultaneously optimizing for accuracy and key hardware metrics (energy, latency, memory). The search space includes dynamic pruning modules and explainability-driven architectural components (e.g., attention mechanisms) to enable interpretable climate feature extraction. After initial NAS, an adaptive pruning stage allows runtime tuning of the emulator’s complexity based on changing energy budgets or sensor data quality, maintaining accuracy-resource tradeoffs over deployment.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on three benchmark climate datasets: ERA5 reanalysis data, CORDEX regional climate projections, and the CMIP6 multi-model ensemble outputs. Baselines include: 1) hardware-oblivious deep emulators like U-Net and TCN models, 2) the prior NAS method “Neural Architecture Search for Low-Power Embedded Climate Emulators,” and 3) a state-of-the-art explainable emulator (e.g., uncertainty-aware U-Net). Metrics are mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean squared error (RMSE) on climate variable prediction (target: >10% reduction in MAE over baselines), on-device inference latency (target: <50ms), peak memory usage (<512KB), and energy consumption per inference (<50mJ). Experiments run on ARM Cortex-M4 and Nvidia Jetson Nano boards, with five random seeds for statistical significance. Ablations include removing adaptive pruning and explainability components.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work which either ignores hardware constraints or fixes architectures post-NAS, our framework integrates live embedded device profiling and dynamic pruning into NAS objectives, enabling emulators that are both highly efficient and adaptable at runtime. This design also incorporates architectural explainability modules, facilitating interpretable climate feature learning, a capability absent in previous black-box low-power emulators.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a probabilistic deep learning climate emulator that performs calibrated, real-time uncertainty quantification across multiple climate regimes and data qualities, enabling operational climate forecasting with fast and trustworthy probabilistic outputs.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning climate emulators accelerate model inference but lack reliable, real-time uncertainty quantification, limiting their operational utility in decision-critical forecasting where risk-aware decisions depend on trustworthy confidence intervals. Existing approaches produce either slow or poorly calibrated uncertainty estimates, and do not maintain calibration across varying climate regimes and input data quality, leading to diminished trust and usability in practical forecasting workflows.

APPROACH: We propose a novel probabilistic emulator architecture combining deep ensemble U-Nets with Bayesian last-layer inference and heteroscedastic quantile regression losses to produce fast, calibrated uncertainty estimates on regional climate downscaling tasks. The method incorporates domain-adaptive uncertainty calibration layers to maintain calibration under distribution shifts between historical and future climate regimes. Efficient uncertainty propagation is enabled via a mixture density output layer trained end-to-end with a hybrid loss balancing sharpness and calibration metrics. Calibration is dynamically updated online using a lightweight Bayesian update mechanism during inference to adapt to varying data quality and real-time input updates.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on benchmark regional climate simulation datasets — CORDEX-CORE for regional climate model outputs, CMIP6 historical and scenario runs, and the ERA5 reanalysis dataset for validation. Baselines include Deep Ensemble U-Net (Uncertainty Quantification for Surface Ozone Emulators, 2025), Bayesian U-Net, and Quantile Regression U-Net models. Metrics: achieve at least 3% improvement in Continuous Ranked Probability Score (CRPS), reduce calibration error (Expected Calibration Error, ECE) below 5%, and reduce inference latency by 50% compared to Bayesian U-Net. Experiments run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a training budget of ~100 GPU-hours, averaging over 5 random seeds with ablations on calibration layers and uncertainty propagation methods.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that either produces slow Bayesian inference or uncalibrated uncertainty in climate emulators, this approach delivers real-time, fully calibrated probabilistic forecasts with an adaptive calibration mechanism that maintains trustworthiness across climate regimes and data qualities — enabling operational use in decision-critical climate forecasting where previous methods fail to provide reliable uncertainty quantification.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hierarchical, memory-adaptive PINN emulator architecture that integrates multi-fidelity climate data via progressive knowledge distillation and soft physics regularization, enabling sub-100MB edge deployment with improved regional accuracy and calibrated uncertainty quantification.

BACKGROUND:  
Current memory-efficient PINN emulators for climate model acceleration achieve physical fidelity under 2% violation error but lack adaptive resolution mechanisms and robust uncertainty quantification critical for heterogeneous regional downscaling tasks on edge devices. Existing deep learning emulators either compromise physical constraint enforcement for model size or fail to generalize across varying spatial scales and emission scenarios, limiting practical edge deployment for mobile climate applications.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-level PINN architecture that dynamically adjusts model complexity and memory usage based on regional climate variability, combining soft physics-informed loss with uncertainty-aware Bayesian distillation from multi-fidelity teacher models trained on coarse and fine ERA5 and CORDEX datasets. The model employs differentiable physics regularizers to maintain physical consistency while a hierarchical student network progressively learns distilled representations, enabling targeted memory reduction below 100MB on NVIDIA Jetson Nano. We further incorporate quantile regression layers to produce calibrated uncertainty estimates alongside standard predictions.

EXPERIMENT:  
Train and evaluate on ERA5 reanalysis, CORDEX regional downscaling datasets, and CMIP6 historical simulations; compare against baseline PINNs from the winning method (“Memory-Efficient PINN Architectures with Soft Physics Regularization”), Physics-Informed Neural Networks (Raissi et al. 2019), and DeepEmulator (from recent RCM emulation studies). Metrics include physics violation error (<2%), region-specific RMSE (targeting >5% reduction over baselines), uncertainty calibration error (e.g., expected calibration error <0.05), and peak memory usage (<100MB) on Jetson Nano hardware. Experiments run with 5 seeds, ablations on multi-fidelity distillation and uncertainty components, and training limited to 48 GPU-hours on A100 hardware.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work which applies uniform soft physics constraints and fixed architectures, this method introduces hierarchical memory adaptation and multi-fidelity knowledge distillation tailored for heterogeneous regional climate patterns, achieving superior physical fidelity and uncertainty quantification at strict edge memory budgets. This enables practical, explainable climate emulation deployments on low-resource devices, a capability absent from existing PINN and deep learning emulator frameworks.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA: Develop a meta-learned, energy-aware neural architecture search (NAS) framework that jointly optimizes for emulator accuracy, uncertainty quantification, and adaptive energy consumption on edge devices, enabling robust, explainable climate model emulators deployable on autonomous sensor networks with strict power budgets.

BACKGROUND: Current deep learning emulators for climate models either focus solely on predictive accuracy or incorporate limited energy-awareness without jointly addressing uncertainty quantification and adaptive device-level energy constraints. This results in models that either cannot run efficiently on low-power autonomous climate sensors or fail to provide reliable uncertainty estimates critical for climate decision-making under variability. There is a need for a unified approach that discovers architectures optimized for accuracy, uncertainty, and energy efficiency simultaneously, ensuring deployment feasibility in remote environments.

APPROACH: We propose a meta-learned NAS framework integrating energy profiling from ARM Cortex-M and NVIDIA Jetson Nano devices as hardware-in-the-loop feedback during search. The search objective simultaneously optimizes (1) emulator accuracy on spatiotemporal climate variables, (2) uncertainty quantification via Bayesian neural network surrogate layers, and (3) device-level energy consumption with real-time adaptive pruning and quantization to guarantee energy budgets. A meta-learning component enables rapid adaptation across diverse climate datasets and hardware profiles, while explainability modules (e.g., SHAP-based attribution) are embedded for model interpretability.

EXPERIMENT: Evaluate on the WeatherBench 2018 dataset, ERA5 reanalysis data, and the ARM ML Sensor Dataset. Baselines include ARM Cortex-M energy-aware NAS from the winning paper, U-Net Bayesian uncertainty models, and standard deep learning emulators like DeepSD and ConvLSTM. Metrics: achieve emulator RMSE reduction >10% over baselines, uncertainty calibration with Expected Calibration Error (ECE) <5%, and energy consumption reduction ≥30% compared to existing energy-aware NAS methods. Protocol: train on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of 500 GPU-hours, deploy selected emulators on ARM Cortex-M4 and Jetson Nano hardware for energy profiling; perform 5 random seeds per experiment and ablation studies isolating energy vs uncertainty vs accuracy objectives.

NOVELTY: Unlike prior work that treats energy efficiency and emulator accuracy or uncertainty quantification in isolation, this method is the first to meta-learn architectures jointly optimized for accuracy, uncertainty, and strict device-level energy constraints with hardware-in-the-loop feedback, enabling explainable, adaptive climate emulators suitable for continuous deployment on autonomous, power-limited sensor networks.

topic: Deep learning emulators for climate model acceleration
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a neural architecture search (NAS)-optimized ultra-compact deep learning emulator framework with integrated noise-robust training and adaptive precision quantization for deployment on resource-constrained embedded ocean sensors, enabling real-time, low-power climate model acceleration with uncertainty quantification.

BACKGROUND:  
Current deep learning emulators for climate and ocean monitoring models suffer from excessive size and power consumption, preventing their deployment on embedded in-situ sensors that must operate under strict memory, compute, and energy constraints. Existing climate emulators lack robustness to sensor noise and uncertainty quantification, critical for reliable real-time environmental monitoring. There is a pressing need for compact, efficient emulators that maintain high predictive accuracy, adapt to noisy inputs, and provide uncertainty estimates for trustworthy field operation.

APPROACH:  
This study proposes a NAS pipeline tailored to embedded hardware constraints (e.g., ARM Cortex-M or low-power ASICs) to evolve model architectures minimizing parameters and operations, combined with noise-augmentation training to enhance robustness to real-world sensor variability. The framework incorporates adaptive precision quantization aware training (QAT) to balance accuracy and efficiency, and integrates lightweight Bayesian layers or evidential deep learning modules for uncertainty quantification. The resulting emulators will be benchmarked for predictive accuracy, robustness to synthetic sensor noise, latency, memory footprint, power consumption, and uncertainty calibration.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will use the CMIP6 climate projection dataset for emulator training and testing regional ocean variables, the MODIS ocean color dataset for real-world sensor noise simulation, and the NOAA Global Ocean Data Assimilation System (GODAS) reanalysis data for validation. Baselines include the DeepESN, U-Net (SOTA emulator), and MobileNetV3 architectures adapted for climate emulation. Metrics measured will be mean absolute error (MAE) and root mean square error (RMSE) (targeting >10% MAE reduction vs baselines), model size (<1MB target), inference latency (<50ms on ARM Cortex-M7), power consumption (measured on representative embedded boards aiming for <100mW), and uncertainty calibration metrics such as expected calibration error (ECE) with target ECE <5%. The experimental protocol includes training on an NVIDIA A100 with a 48-hour compute budget, evaluation across 5 random seeds, and ablation studies on NAS search space, noise augmentation levels, and quantization strategies.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work that focuses solely on emulator accuracy or downscaling functions, this research simultaneously addresses extreme embedded hardware constraints, noise robustness, and uncertainty quantification in a unified NAS framework. This approach enables deployment of reliable, low-power climate emulators directly on in-situ ocean sensors, a capability absent from existing large or power-intensive models and beyond the scope of prior climate emulator and NAS studies.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Adaptive Latency-Aware Personalized Federated Aggregation with Hierarchical Client Clustering for Heterogeneous Non-IID Data.

BACKGROUND:  
Federated learning with heterogeneous clients and non-IID data suffers from inefficient aggregation due to latency variability and data distribution skews, often causing slow convergence and suboptimal personalization. Existing adaptive weighting schemes, such as "Adaptive Weighted Aggregation under Latency Constraints," account for latency but treat clients independently without structural grouping, limiting scalability and the ability to exploit similarity among clients’ data heterogeneity and latency profiles. The open problem is how to jointly optimize latency-aware aggregation and personalization in large-scale, heterogeneous client populations while addressing non-IID data distributions more effectively.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical client clustering mechanism that groups clients based on both latency profiles and statistical similarity of their local data distributions measured via Wasserstein distance on feature embeddings. Within each cluster, adaptive weighted aggregation dynamically prioritizes client updates by jointly considering latency, computational speed, and cluster-specific data heterogeneity to improve personalization. A two-level aggregation is then performed: first intra-cluster aggregation to reduce noise and communication overhead, followed by inter-cluster aggregation with latency-aware weighting to form the global model. This method explicitly integrates latency feedback and statistical clustering to optimize accuracy-latency trade-offs and personalized model performance across heterogeneous mobile edge devices.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate the approach on FEMNIST, LEAF (Sent140), and CIFAR-10 Non-IID partitioned datasets, comparing against baselines including FedAvg, FedProx, and the original Adaptive Weighted Aggregation under Latency Constraints method. Metrics include test accuracy per client (personalization), average end-to-end communication latency (targeting >15% reduction vs. Adaptive Weighted Aggregation), and convergence speed (number of rounds to reach 90% of peak accuracy). Experiments will run on a simulated edge environment with 100 clients, using three random seeds and conducting ablation studies to isolate the effects of clustering and latency weighting.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike existing methods that treat latency-aware weighting and personalization independently or uniformly across heterogeneous clients, this approach integrates hierarchical clustering based on both latency and data distribution similarity, enabling scalable, latency-sensitive aggregation that significantly improves personalization accuracy and reduces communication overhead in highly heterogeneous, non-IID federated environments.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a personalized federated learning framework with adaptive resilient aggregation that explicitly optimizes for asynchronous updates and client dropout in heterogeneous edge IoT environments, incorporating lightweight model partitioning and dynamic communication scheduling to minimize energy and latency while maintaining high personalization accuracy on non-IID data.

BACKGROUND:  
Edge IoT devices face severe resource constraints including limited compute power, intermittent connectivity, and strict energy budgets, resulting in frequent client dropouts and highly asynchronous updates that degrade federated learning performance. Current state-of-the-art FL methods either impose high communication overhead or lack robustness to unstable connectivity and device heterogeneity, limiting their applicability for personalized models on edge IoT. The open problem is to design an FL approach that simultaneously ensures robustness to client dropouts, asynchronous updates, and extreme resource constraints while preserving model personalization quality on heterogeneous and non-IID data.

APPROACH:  
The proposed method, called **Adaptive Resilient Personalized Aggregation (ARPA)**, integrates (1) a lightweight split-model architecture where computationally heavy layers run on the edge server while clients perform minimal local updates tailored to their hardware capacity; (2) a dynamic client scheduling and communication protocol that prioritizes clients based on connectivity patterns and energy states to reduce redundant transmissions; (3) an asynchronous aggregation algorithm that weights client updates by freshness and dropout probability, explicitly modeling client unreliability to maintain stable global and personalized models; and (4) a dual-distillation mechanism where global knowledge is softly distilled into personalized client models and vice versa to bridge statistical heterogeneity without heavy overhead.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate ARPA on three benchmark datasets: **CIFAR-100** (image classification with natural non-IID splits), **FEMNIST** (heterogeneous handwriting recognition mimicking IoT edge clients), and **Google Speech Commands v2** (audio recognition with realistic user device variability). Compare against baselines including **HeteroSFL**, **FedGKD**, and **Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation** under simulated intermittent connectivity and dropout settings. Measure metrics including top-1 accuracy (>3% improvement over baselines), communication rounds to convergence (reduce by ≥25%), and per-client energy consumption (reduce by ≥20%). Perform experiments on edge hardware simulators with compute budgets limited to single-board computers (e.g., Raspberry Pi 4) and run 5 random seeds with comprehensive ablation studies on aggregation weights and scheduling policies.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that either neglect intermittent connectivity and client dropout (e.g., FedGKD) or focus solely on communication reduction without modeling client unreliability (e.g., HeteroSFL), ARPA explicitly incorporates adaptive aggregation weighted by dropout probability and freshness, combined with split-model design and dynamic scheduling, enabling robust personalized FL on severely constrained and unstable edge IoT devices with non-IID data, a capability missing from existing methods.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
A hierarchical federated learning framework for IoT sensor networks that dynamically forms energy-aware, proximity-based clusters using lightweight graph neural networks to optimize rapid cluster formation and cluster-level model aggregation under stringent bandwidth, latency, and energy constraints, explicitly addressing heterogeneous and non-IID data distributions.

BACKGROUND:  
In large-scale IoT sensor networks, federated learning faces unique challenges: sensors have limited communication bandwidth and energy, connectivity is sporadic, and data distributions are highly heterogeneous and non-IID. Existing clustering and aggregation methods either do not adapt quickly to dynamic network conditions or incur excessive energy consumption and latency, leading to slow convergence and poor model performance in real-time applications.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel hierarchical FL system that uses a lightweight graph neural network (GNN) at the edge servers to dynamically detect and form clusters based on spatiotemporal sensor similarity, communication quality, and residual energy levels. Cluster-level aggregation employs an adaptive compression scheme and asynchronous update protocol tuned by a multi-objective optimization algorithm balancing convergence speed, energy usage, and communication overhead. Our method explicitly incorporates sensor data heterogeneity by weighting client contributions within clusters using learned data distribution embeddings, thus mitigating non-IID effects during aggregation.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will evaluate on real-world IoT benchmark datasets: Intel Lab Data for environmental sensing, N-BaIoT for botnet attack detection, and the USC-HAD human activity dataset. Baselines include HeteroSFL, FedGKD, and the Joint Client Scheduling and Wireless Resource Allocation (SC) algorithm. Metrics will measure >3% absolute improvement in global model accuracy, >25% reduction in cluster formation latency, and >30% decrease in energy consumption averaged per training round. Experiments will be conducted on a wireless testbed simulating IoT edge nodes with limited bandwidth and power, using 5 independent runs with ablation studies on cluster formation and aggregation components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods that treat clustering and aggregation separately or assume static cluster formation, our framework integrates dynamic, energy-aware clustering via GNNs with data-distribution-weighted aggregation, enabling rapid and efficient federated learning in highly constrained and heterogeneous IoT sensor networks under non-IID data — capabilities absent in existing methods like HeteroSFL or SC scheduling.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
A federated graph learning framework that integrates dynamic schema embedding alignment with hierarchical secure multi-party computation and adaptive client clustering to robustly handle heterogeneous and non-IID cross-institutional data while minimizing communication overhead and preserving strict privacy guarantees.

BACKGROUND:  
Current federated learning methods struggle to jointly address schema heterogeneity, statistical non-IIDness, and strict privacy constraints across organizations with diverse data structures. Existing approaches either incur excessive communication costs or expose information leakage risks, limiting their applicability in sensitive domains such as healthcare and finance where legal compliance and data format differences are critical. Furthermore, client heterogeneity and non-IID data distributions degrade model convergence and accuracy, creating an unmet need for scalable, privacy-preserving, and schema-heterogeneity-aware federated graph learning solutions.

APPROACH:  
We propose SchemaAlign-SFL, a federated graph learning framework that first employs a dynamic schema embedding alignment module to learn a shared latent representation space across heterogeneous organizational data schemas without revealing schema details. This is combined with a hierarchical secure multi-party computation (MPC) protocol that partitions clients into adaptive clusters based on schema and data similarity, reducing expensive encrypted communication to cluster-level aggregation. Within each cluster, encrypted collaborative training proceeds using split federated learning (SFL) tailored to graph neural networks, mitigating non-IID effects via intra-cluster knowledge distillation and inter-cluster consistency regularization. The framework integrates communication-efficient compression and asynchronous updates to further reduce latency and bandwidth usage.

EXPERIMENT:  
Validation will be conducted on cross-institutional graph datasets with natural schema variance and non-IID data including MIMIC-III (healthcare patient records graph), the Financial Industry Business Ontology (FIBO)-annotated transaction graph dataset, and the Open Graph Benchmark’s ogbl-collab dataset adapted with synthetic schema heterogeneity. Baselines include Schema-Heterogeneity Aware Federated Graph Learning (SH-FGL), HeteroSFL, and FedGKD. Evaluation metrics include test accuracy (target >3% absolute improvement over SH-FGL), schema alignment quality measured by embedding cosine similarity (>0.9 intra-cluster consistency), privacy leakage quantified under honest-but-curious and active adversary threat models (leakage <0.01 bits per protocol run), and communication overhead reduction (>25% less bandwidth than SH-FGL). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds, including ablation studies on clustering, MPC hierarchy, and knowledge distillation components.

NOVELTY:  
This approach meaningfully extends beyond SH-FGL and prior work by simultaneously addressing multi-level client heterogeneity — schema, data distribution, and device heterogeneity — through adaptive clustering combined with hierarchical MPC, which prior methods either ignore or treat monolithically. Unlike HeteroSFL or FedGKD that focus primarily on non-IID client data without schema alignment or privacy beyond encryption, SchemaAlign-SFL enables scalable, privacy-preserving federated learning over heterogeneous graph data with provable privacy guarantees and communication efficiency, directly tackling the open problem of legally compliant cross-institutional model training on non-standardized, non-IID graph-structured data.

topic: Federated learning with heterogeneous and non-IID data
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a federated learning framework integrating adaptive hierarchical model pruning with dynamic client clustering based on both data distribution heterogeneity and device memory constraints, enabling personalized, communication-efficient training on ultra-low memory IoT devices while maintaining robust accuracy on highly non-IID data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current federated learning methods struggle to deliver personalized models on heterogeneous clients, especially low-end IoT devices with severe memory constraints (<50MB), due to large model sizes and non-IID data distributions causing client drift and poor convergence. Existing personalized aggregation approaches lack joint adaptation to both memory budgets and statistical heterogeneity at fine-grained cluster levels, leading to suboptimal accuracy or infeasible deployment on resource-constrained devices.

APPROACH:  
We propose Hierarchical Pruned Personalized Aggregation (HiPPA), which first clusters clients dynamically by their memory capacity and local data distribution similarity using a dual-metric embedding. Within each cluster, a lightweight adaptive pruning mechanism identifies a cluster-specific subnetwork by pruning redundant weights informed by both resource limits and data-driven importance scores. Federated aggregation then occurs on these pruned subnetworks per cluster, enabling personalized models within tight memory budgets. Additionally, cross-cluster knowledge distillation is employed periodically to mitigate client drift and enhance global generalization without increasing model size or communication overhead.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate HiPPA on three heterogeneous, non-IID federated benchmarks: CIFAR-100 (fine-grained class splits), FEMNIST (handwritten character recognition), and Shakespeare (next-character prediction from text by author). Baselines include Memory-Constrained Personalized Aggregation with Model Pruning and Client Clustering (the winning approach), FedGKD, and Adaptive Dual-Mode Distillation. Metrics focus on test accuracy improvement (>3% absolute over baselines), peak memory usage reduction (>40%), and communication cost reduction (>25%) measured across 5 independent runs with systematic ablation on clustering granularity and pruning thresholds. Experiments run on edge device simulators with memory capped at 50MB and typical IoT CPU constraints.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, HiPPA jointly and dynamically adapts model pruning and client clustering based on heterogeneous data distributions and device memory constraints, not just resource profiles alone. It further integrates cross-cluster knowledge distillation to address client drift, enabling communication- and memory-efficient personalized federated learning on ultra-low-memory IoT devices with non-IID data—capabilities missing from existing pruning or split learning schemes.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a causal foundation model that explicitly learns gene regulatory mechanisms under diverse perturbation regimes by integrating single-cell CRISPR screening datasets with multi-modal genomic data, enabling robust and generalizable inference of gene regulatory networks that disentangle direct and indirect effects even under severe distributional shifts.

BACKGROUND:  
Current gene regulatory network (GRN) inference methods, including recent causal foundation models, struggle to reliably disambiguate direct causal relationships from indirect associations in the presence of complex perturbations such as multiplexed CRISPR screens. Existing models fail to generalize across diverse experimental conditions due to distributional shifts introduced by interventions, resulting in confounded or spurious network reconstructions. There is an urgent need for a foundation model trained on large-scale, multimodal single-cell perturbation data that can robustly infer causal regulatory interactions, facilitating accurate biological interpretation and therapeutic target discovery.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-modal causal foundation model that jointly encodes single-cell transcriptomic responses from CRISPR perturbation datasets (e.g., Perturb-seq, CROP-seq), chromatin accessibility profiles (scATAC-seq), and epigenetic marks to learn a unified latent causal representation of gene regulation. The model leverages invariant causal prediction principles and domain adaptation techniques to explicitly model distributional shifts induced by perturbations, separating direct gene-gene regulatory effects from indirect downstream changes. A graph neural network backbone with attention mechanisms integrates multi-omic features and perturbation metadata to predict intervention-specific causal edges, trained end-to-end using contrastive and causal regularization losses. We incorporate a perturbation-aware encoder that models combinatorial and dose-dependent intervention effects for generalization beyond single perturbations.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark on Perturb-seq datasets of immune cells (e.g., Norman et al. 2019), CROP-seq datasets for cancer cell lines (Datlinger et al. 2017), and the ENCODE single-cell multi-omic data linking scRNA-seq and scATAC-seq profiles. Baseline comparisons include GRNBoost2, Scribe, and the existing causal foundation model from the tournament winner paper. Metrics evaluated will be area under the precision-recall curve (AUPR) and F1-score for causal edge identification (targeting >5% absolute improvement over baselines), calibration of causal effect sizes (expected reduction in bias >10%), and robustness of inferred networks under held-out perturbation regimes measured by domain generalization gap (<0.1 drop in AUPR). Experiments will be run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a compute budget of ~50,000 GPU hours, using 5 random seeds per setting and ablation studies on multi-modality integration and causal regularization components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior foundation models that treat perturbation data as static or rely on correlation-based inference, this approach explicitly models causal mechanisms under complex, combinatorial perturbation-induced distributional shifts by integrating multi-modal single-cell data and applying invariant causal prediction and domain adaptation. This enables robust disentanglement of direct regulatory interactions from indirect effects and generalization beyond experimental conditions represented in training data, a capability not addressed by existing single-cell foundation models or causal network inference methods.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a consistency-regularized domain-adversarial foundation model that aligns multimodal single-cell embeddings across heterogeneous batches by explicitly disentangling batch-specific variation from biological signals, enabling robust cross-batch and cross-modality integration with demonstrable improvements in cell type classification and batch mixing metrics.

BACKGROUND:  
Current pretrained foundation models for single-cell genomics suffer from batch-induced representation drift, where non-biological technical variation between sequencing runs, protocols, or experimental centers causes embedding instability. This severely limits the integration of large-scale atlases spanning multiple modalities (e.g., RNA, ATAC, protein) and batches, undermining downstream analyses such as cell type classification and trajectory inference. Existing domain adversarial approaches partially address batch effects but lack explicit regularization to stabilize embeddings and guarantee consistency across batches and modalities within a single foundation model.

APPROACH:  
We propose to extend the current consistency-regularized multimodal representation learning framework by (1) introducing a dual-branch encoder architecture that separately models biological latent space and batch-specific nuisance factors via disentangled variational inference; (2) applying a novel consistency loss that enforces embedding invariance under batch perturbations and modality masking; and (3) leveraging domain adversarial training to adversarially remove batch-specific signals from the biological latent space. This integrated approach will produce stable, batch-agnostic multimodal embeddings that retain biological information critical for downstream tasks.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will benchmark our method on the Human Cell Atlas (HCA) v2 multimodal dataset (including scRNA-seq + scATAC-seq + CITE-seq protein), the Mouse Cell Atlas (MCA) multimodal dataset, and the 10x Multiome PBMC dataset. Baselines will include Consistency-Regularized Multimodal Representation Learning Across Batches (the tournament winner), totalVI, scVI, and Harmony. Evaluation metrics will focus on batch integration quality (using LISI score with a target >10% relative improvement), downstream cell type classification accuracy (>3% absolute F1 score gain), and latent space stability measured by silhouette scores (>0.1 improvement). Experiments will be performed on GPUs (NVIDIA A100), with a compute budget of ~1200 GPU hours, running five random seeds for robustness and ablation studies isolating the effects of the disentanglement module and consistency loss.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior domain adversarial or consistency-regularized models, our method explicitly disentangles batch nuisance from biological signals within the foundation model’s latent space, combined with a novel dual-branch encoder architecture and embedding consistency enforced across multiple modalities. This enables stable, biologically meaningful embeddings that prior methods cannot guarantee, overcoming batch-induced drift at scale in large multimodal single-cell atlases.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a hybrid graph neural network framework that integrates uncertainty-aware edge inference with multi-view single-cell omics embeddings to robustly reconstruct regulatory networks from extremely sparse and noisy single-cell data.

BACKGROUND:  
Current single-cell regulatory network inference methods fail to recover biologically meaningful interactions when data sparsity and noise create missing or uncertain edges in the cellular regulatory graph. This limitation prevents accurate modeling of cell-type specific regulation in low-data regimes, hindering the interpretability and utility of single-cell foundation models for downstream biological insights. The open problem is to design algorithms that explicitly model and leverage uncertainty in partial observations to reconstruct complete and robust regulatory networks under extreme sparsity.

APPROACH:  
We propose a two-stage framework: first, a probabilistic edge imputation module based on Bayesian graph neural networks (BGNN) estimates edge existence probabilities and confidence intervals using multi-view embeddings derived from single-cell RNA-seq, ATAC-seq, and spatial transcriptomics data. Second, an uncertainty-aware graph transformer refines regulatory graph structure by propagating information weighted by edge uncertainties, jointly optimizing node and edge representations. This approach explicitly models missing data and edge uncertainty, enabling biologically plausible network reconstruction despite high sparsity. Training includes multitask supervision from curated regulatory edges and integrates contrastive learning to enforce global graph consistency.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on three benchmark datasets with regulatory ground truths: (1) PBMC 10x Multiome dataset (paired scRNA+scATAC), (2) Mouse Brain Single-cell Multi-omics Atlas (scRNA+spatial), and (3) Human Hematopoietic Stem Cell Atlas. Baselines include GRNBoost2, PIDC, and the recent DeepMAPS graph model. Metrics: Area Under Precision-Recall Curve (AUPR) for edge recovery (target >5% absolute improvement over best baseline), F1-score for cell-type specific regulatory edge classification (target >3% absolute gain), and edge uncertainty calibration error (ECE) reduction by at least 10%. Perform five independent runs per dataset with fixed seeds, conduct ablation studies removing uncertainty modeling and multi-view inputs.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior methods that treat missing edges as zeros or ignore uncertainty, our framework explicitly models edge existence uncertainty via Bayesian inference and propagates it through the graph transformer, enabling robust reconstruction in extreme sparsity. It uniquely leverages multi-modal single-cell data integration within a unified uncertainty-aware architecture, improving interpretability and biological plausibility of inferred regulatory networks beyond current single-cell foundation models and graph neural approaches.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a personalized, multi-omics foundation model that predicts interpretable patient-specific latent biological states under hypothetical perturbations with uncertainty quantification, enabling robust in-silico simulation of individualized therapeutic interventions.

BACKGROUND:  
Current perturbation prediction models lack precision and robustness when applied to limited data from individual patients, failing to capture patient heterogeneity and provide actionable clinical insights. Moreover, existing single-cell foundation models and multi-omics integration frameworks do not adequately quantify uncertainty or interpret latent biological states in the context of personalized medicine, limiting their translational utility for simulating drug and genetic perturbations.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hierarchical multi-modal transformer-based foundation model trained on integrated multi-omics and single-cell datasets that learns interpretable latent variables reflecting patient-specific cellular states. The model incorporates Bayesian layers for uncertainty quantification and uses counterfactual learning to simulate in-silico perturbations such as drug treatments or gene knockouts at single-cell resolution. Interpretability is enhanced by disentangling latent factors aligned with known biological pathways and clinical phenotypes, facilitating actionable predictions tailored to individual patient profiles.

EXPERIMENT:  
We will train and evaluate the model on the Human Cell Atlas (HCA) single-cell multi-omics dataset, the Cancer Genome Atlas (TCGA) bulk multi-omics and clinical data, and the Perturb-seq dataset for CRISPR-based gene perturbations. Baselines include CPA (Compositional Perturbation Autoencoder), scGen, and totalVI. Evaluation metrics will measure perturbation prediction accuracy (R² > 0.75 improvement over baselines), uncertainty calibration (expected calibration error reduction >15%), and clinical relevance quantified via drug response prediction AUROC (>5% absolute improvement). Experiments will be conducted on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with a 2M token training budget, 5 random seeds, and ablation studies isolating uncertainty quantification and latent interpretability components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior single-cell foundation models and perturbation predictors, this approach uniquely integrates multi-omics data with Bayesian uncertainty estimation and disentangled, clinically interpretable latent variables, enabling precise, robust, and actionable in-silico perturbation simulations personalized to individual patient datasets, which prior work neither achieves nor quantitatively validates under realistic data constraints.

topic: Foundation models for genomics and single-cell biology
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a compressed, hardware-aware masked token prediction foundation model for real-time single-cell RNA sequencing on edge devices by integrating structured weight pruning, knowledge distillation, and dynamic token masking to achieve sub-100ms latency with minimal accuracy loss.

BACKGROUND:  
Current large transformer-based models for masked token prediction in single-cell RNA-seq analysis are computationally intensive, making real-time inference on edge devices infeasible due to latency exceeding sequencing throughput requirements. Existing compression techniques are either generic or offline optimized, failing to meet strict real-time constraints in streaming single-cell workflows. There is a critical need for a purpose-built compression strategy that preserves imputation accuracy while enabling deployment in resource-constrained edge environments for streaming single-cell data.

APPROACH:  
We propose a multi-stage compression pipeline combining structured weight pruning tailored to transformer layers, knowledge distillation from a large pretrained masked token prediction model to a lightweight student, and a novel dynamic token masking scheme that prioritizes biologically informative gene tokens during inference. This is coupled with hardware-aware quantization and edge-specific runtime optimizations targeting ARM Cortex-A processors common in sequencing devices. The model is trained and distilled on large-scale single-cell RNA-seq data, with iterative pruning schedules that maintain downstream imputation fidelity.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on the Tabula Muris Senis, Human Cell Atlas (HCA) immune cell subset, and 10x Genomics PBMC 68k datasets. Baselines include scBERT (a single-cell transformer masked token predictor), DistilBERT, and EdgeBERT compressed with generic pruning and quantization. Metrics comprise imputation accuracy (R² and RMSE) on masked gene expression tokens, runtime latency per inference batch (target <100ms), and model size (MB). Experiments run on ARM Cortex-A72 edge hardware with 3 independent seeds per setting. Ablations will isolate effects of pruning, distillation, and dynamic masking.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior foundational single-cell models or generic compression methods, this approach uniquely optimizes compression specifically for masked token prediction in streaming single-cell RNA-seq on edge devices, incorporating dynamic token masking that exploits biological gene importance for accuracy-latency trade-offs. This enables real-time inference under strict latency constraints (<100ms) which prior large transformer models and compression baselines cannot achieve.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a Dynamic Context-Aware Compression and Attention Framework (DCCAF) for emergent communication in multi-agent systems that jointly optimizes message content selection and adaptive compression under real-time network and task constraints to maximize communication efficiency and cooperative task success.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication methods in multi-agent systems do not adequately address the joint challenges of bandwidth constraints and task-relevance under dynamic network conditions, especially in realistic environments where network topology and agent goals co-evolve. Existing dynamic attention models, such as those applied in MANETs, focus on bandwidth and network volatility but do not integrate adaptive message compression or goal-oriented communication protocols that emerge during learning. This results in suboptimal communication efficiency and reduced cooperative task performance when agents must communicate under strict bandwidth and latency constraints.

APPROACH:  
We propose DCCAF, which extends dynamic attention weighting with a learned, context-aware compression module that encodes messages into compact representations based on both network state embeddings and evolving multi-agent task demands. The method uses a neural architecture that combines: (1) a dynamic attention mechanism that modulates message importance using real-time network volatility and agent goal embeddings, and (2) an adaptive compression layer that selectively compresses or expands message payloads to optimize bandwidth use without sacrificing task-relevant information. The system is trained end-to-end via multi-agent reinforcement learning, encouraging emergent protocols that balance communication cost and utility under operational constraints.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate on MANETSim (for network volatility and bandwidth constraints), Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MAPE) with cooperative navigation and communication tasks, and the StarCraft Multi-Agent Challenge (SMAC) for complex strategic coordination. Metrics include communication bandwidth utilization (target ≥15% reduction vs. static attention baselines), task success rate improvements (≥10% absolute increase over baselines), and message relevance scores (≥30% improvement). Baselines include: Dynamic Attention Weighting under Bandwidth Constraints, CommNet, and TarMAC. Experiments run on 8 NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 seeds each; ablation studies will isolate the impact of dynamic compression vs. dynamic attention alone.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior works that separately address either dynamic attention weighting or emergent communication protocols without integrated compression, DCCAF uniquely combines adaptive message compression with dynamic attention under real-time multi-agent task and network contexts. This enables emergent communication protocols that are simultaneously bandwidth-efficient, task-goal oriented, and robust to network volatility, a capability absent from previous MANET-focused or emergent communication frameworks.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a robust emergent communication framework for multi-agent healthcare systems that integrates ontology-grounded symbolic encoding with adaptive error-correcting codes and differential privacy mechanisms to ensure reliable, interpretable, and privacy-preserving coordination under noisy channel conditions.

BACKGROUND:  
Multi-agent healthcare systems require interpretable and reliable communication protocols to coordinate patient care effectively. Current emergent communication methods inadequately address the combined challenges of message ambiguity from noisy channels, privacy constraints imposed by sensitive medical data, and grounding communication in rich, structured medical ontologies. Existing approaches either lack integrated error resilience aligned with medical knowledge or fail to simultaneously guarantee differential privacy, leading to critical miscommunications and privacy risks.

APPROACH:  
We propose a hybrid communication architecture where agents encode messages by grounding symbols explicitly in medical ontologies such as SNOMED CT and UMLS, then apply ontology-aware adaptive error-correcting codes tailored to the semantic structure of these ontologies. This coding dynamically adjusts redundancy based on channel noise estimates while preserving message interpretability. Simultaneously, a differential privacy mechanism customized for multi-agent healthcare scenarios is integrated to obfuscate sensitive information without degrading communication fidelity. Agents learn to optimize communication policies end-to-end with multi-task objectives balancing task success, message error rates, and privacy budgets.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluation will be conducted on the MIMIC-III clinical dataset, the eICU Collaborative Research Database, and the PhysioNet Challenge 2019 dataset, simulating realistic noisy communication channels typical in distributed healthcare IoT environments. Baselines include the original Error-Resilient Symbol Grounding approach, Differentially Private Federated Learning (DP-FedAvg), and Neuro-Spike Communication frameworks. Metrics are task-specific coordination accuracy (>5% absolute improvement), communication error rate (<5%), and privacy leakage measured by epsilon in differential privacy (target ε < 1.0). Experiments will run on NVIDIA A100 GPUs with 5 random seeds, with ablations isolating the effects of ontology grounding, error correction, and privacy components.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior work, our method uniquely integrates ontology-aware adaptive error correction with differential privacy in an end-to-end learnable emergent communication system, ensuring simultaneous interpretability, fault tolerance, and strong privacy guarantees—an unaddressed intersection in multi-agent healthcare communication protocols.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Adaptive emergent communication topologies that dynamically restructure local agent interaction networks based on real-time environmental and swarm state feedback to optimize energy efficiency and connectivity resilience during search and rescue operations in partially collapsed, communication-impaired environments.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication approaches in swarm robotics do not adequately handle rapidly fluctuating agent densities, frequent communication blackouts, and strict energy constraints typical in disaster zones such as partially collapsed buildings. These limitations cause failures in maintaining robust coordination and information flow, critically impairing search and rescue effectiveness. The open problem is to engineer communication topologies that adapt in real-time to dynamic connectivity changes and energy budgets to sustain resilient swarm coordination under these harsh conditions.

APPROACH:  
We propose a decentralized algorithm whereby each agent continuously estimates local network parameters (agent density, signal quality, energy reserves) and employs reinforcement learning-augmented policy modules to selectively form, drop, or re-route communication links with nearby agents. The method utilizes a multi-objective optimization framework balancing connectivity robustness (e.g., graph connectivity and redundancy) against energy expenditure, supported by dynamically evolving local topologies rather than static or fully connected graphs. Agents also incorporate neuro-spike-inspired minimal payload signaling for link maintenance to reduce communication overhead during blackouts.

EXPERIMENT:  
Evaluate the approach on the RoboCup Rescue Simulation Benchmark (RCRS 2018), the Multi-Agent Particle Environment (MAPE) Search and Rescue scenarios, and the Habitat-Matterport 3D (HM3D) dataset for navigation in collapsed structures. Metrics include achieving >5% absolute improvement in successful victim localization rates, >30% reduction in communication energy usage compared to fixed-topology baselines like CommNet, TarMAC, and SchedNet, and maintaining >95% network connectivity under induced communication blackouts. Experiments will be run on an NVIDIA A100 cluster with 5 seeds each; ablation studies will isolate the effects of adaptive topology vs fixed topology and neuro-spike signaling.

NOVELTY:  
Unlike prior emergent communication work that focuses on fixed or homogeneous communication protocols (e.g., CommNet, TarMAC) or minimal payload signaling without topology adaptation, our method dynamically restructures the communication graph in response to real-time environmental and swarm state changes, explicitly balancing energy constraints with connectivity resilience in mission-critical, highly dynamic search and rescue settings. This capability to adapt both message content and network structure is currently unaddressed in SOTA.

topic: Emergent communication in multi-agent systems
text:
IDEA:  
Develop a low-latency, jitter-resilient emergent spike-based communication protocol integrating adaptive temporal encoding and decentralized predictive synchronization for real-time multi-agent drone swarms operating under dynamic network topologies.

BACKGROUND:  
Current emergent communication methods for multi-agent drone swarms fail to sufficiently minimize communication latency and timing jitter, which critically degrade swarm formation control and adaptation in fast-changing, high-mobility environments. The open problem is designing an emergent communication protocol that ensures ultra-fast, reliable synchronization with minimal delay variability across heterogeneous agents in dynamic wireless network conditions.

APPROACH:  
We propose a novel communication framework combining ultra-fast neuro-spike signaling with adaptive temporal encoding schemes that adjust spike timing precision based on real-time network delay estimation. Decentralized predictive synchronization algorithms enable each drone to locally anticipate and compensate for latency and jitter without centralized coordination.
